abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) could power real-time assistants and autonomous agents, but they face a critical challenge: understanding near-infinite video streams without escalating latency and memory usage. Processing entire videos with full attention leads to quadratic computational costs and poor performance on long videos. Meanwhile, simple sliding window methods are also flawed, as they either break coherence or suffer from high latency due to redundant recomputation. In this paper, we introduce StreamingVLM, a model designed for real-time, stable understanding of infinite visual input. Our approach is a unified framework that aligns training with streaming inference. During inference, we maintain a compact KV cache by reusing states of attention sinks, a short window of recent vision tokens, and a long window of recent text tokens. This streaming ability is instilled via a simple supervised fine-tuning (SFT) strategy that applies full attention on short, overlapped video chunks, which effectively mimics the inference-time attention pattern without training on prohibitively long contexts. For evaluation, we build Inf-Streams-Eval, a new benchmark with videos averaging over two hours that requires dense, per-second alignment between frames and text. On Inf-Streams-Eval, StreamingVLM achieves a 66.18% win rate against GPT-4O mini and maintains stable, real-time performance at up to 8 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100. Notably, our SFT strategy also enhances general VQA abilities without any VQA-specific fine-tuning, improving performance on LongVideoBench by +4.30 and OVOBench Realtime by +5.96. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: An efficient and asymmetric KV cache management, prioritizing textual context over older visual frames, is crucial for maintaining low and stable latency in continuous video streams.
Aligning the training strategy (overlapped chunks with full attention) with the streaming inference mechanism (sinks, windows) effectively enables models to generalize to unbounded contexts.
Contiguous RoPE is essential for positional stability during token eviction, preventing performance degradation in long-horizon multimodal streaming scenarios.
originalProblem: Traditional Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face quadratic computational costs and memory exhaustion when applied to long, continuous video streams, hindering their real-world deployment.
Existing streaming methods for VLMs either break context coherence through frequent resets or introduce high latency due to redundant recomputation in overlapping windows.
A significant gap exists between VLM training on short, discrete videos and the demands of real-time, infinite streaming inference, leading to performance degradation in deployment scenarios.
results: StreamingVLM achieved a 66.18% win rate against GPT-4o mini on the Inf-Streams-Eval benchmark, demonstrating superior real-time captioning capabilities on videos averaging over two hours.
The model maintains stable, real-time performance at up to 8 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU, exhibiting consistently low and stable per-token latency below 0.1 seconds.

The streaming-focused training strategy also improved general Visual Question Answering (VQA) abilities, showing gains of +4.30 on LongVideoBench and +5.96 on OVOBench Realtime compared to its base model.
solution: StreamingVLM utilizes a streaming-aware KV cache that incorporates fixed attention sinks, a recent text window, and a recent vision window to efficiently manage context and maintain stable performance.
It implements contiguous Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) to prevent positional drift and stabilize long-horizon inference as older tokens are evicted and new ones are added.
A tailored training strategy employs overlapped-chunk, full-attention supervised fine-tuning with interleaved vision-text tokens to align the model with real-time streaming inference patterns.
paper_summary.summary: StreamingVLM, developed by researchers from MIT, NVIDIA, and First Intelligence, is a Vision-Language Model engineered for real-time understanding of infinite video streams. It achieves stable, low-latency performance by employing an efficient KV cache management system and a novel training strategy, resulting in a 66.18% win rate against GPT-4o mini on the Inf-Streams-Eval benchmark while operating at up to 8 frames per second.

title: StreamingVLM: Real-Time Understanding for Infinite Video Streams

abstract:
The rapid growth of online video platforms, particularly live streaming
services, has created an urgent need for real-time video understanding systems.
These systems must process continuous video streams and respond to user queries
instantaneously, presenting unique challenges for current Video Large Language
Models (VideoLLMs). While existing VideoLLMs excel at processing complete
videos, they face significant limitations in streaming scenarios due to their
inability to handle dense, redundant frames efficiently. We introduce
TimeChat-Online, a novel online VideoLLM that revolutionizes real-time video
interaction. At its core lies our innovative Differential Token Drop (DTD)
module, which addresses the fundamental challenge of visual redundancy in
streaming videos. Drawing inspiration from human visual perception's Change
Blindness phenomenon, DTD preserves meaningful temporal changes while filtering
out static, redundant content between frames. Remarkably, our experiments
demonstrate that DTD achieves an 82.8% reduction in video tokens while
maintaining 98% performance on StreamingBench, revealing that over 80% of
visual content in streaming videos is naturally redundant without requiring
language guidance. To enable seamless real-time interaction, we present
TimeChat-Online-139K, a comprehensive streaming video dataset featuring diverse
interaction patterns including backward-tracing, current-perception, and
future-responding scenarios. TimeChat-Online's unique Proactive Response
capability, naturally achieved through continuous monitoring of video scene
transitions via DTD, sets it apart from conventional approaches. Our extensive
evaluation demonstrates TimeChat-Online's superior performance on streaming
benchmarks (StreamingBench and OvOBench) and maintaining competitive results on
long-form video tasks such as Video-MME and MLVU.

keyInsights: Over 80% of visual tokens in streaming videos are naturally redundant and can be dropped without significant performance loss
Scene transitions detected through token drop ratio curves enable proactive response triggering
Position-aware token preservation is crucial for maintaining spatial-temporal understanding
originalProblem: Existing VideoLLMs struggle with processing streaming video due to computational costs of handling dense, redundant frames
Current models cannot provide proactive responses for questions requiring future visual context
results: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on StreamingBench (58.0) and OVO-Bench (47.6)
Reduces video tokens by 84.6% while improving accuracy by 5.7 points on 30-60 minute videos
Maintains competitive performance on general long-form video tasks (VideoMME: 63.3, MLVU: 65.4)
Enables real-time processing and proactive responses through efficient token management
solution: Differential Token Drop (DTD) technique identifies and filters redundant visual content between consecutive frames
Position-aware token dropping with Multi-modal Rotary Position Embedding preserves spatial-temporal relationships
First-in-first-out memory bank stores compressed historical video tokens for efficient processing
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University and collaborating institutions develop TimeChat-Online, a streaming video understanding framework that reduces visual token processing by 82.8% through differential token dropping while maintaining 98% performance on StreamingBench, enabling efficient real-time interaction with continuous video streams.

title: TimeChat-Online: 80% Visual Tokens are Naturally Redundant in Streaming  Videos

abstract: We present LongLive, a frame-level autoregressive (AR) framework for real-time and interactive long video generation. Long video generation presents challenges in both efficiency and quality. Diffusion and Diffusion-Forcing models can produce high-quality videos but suffer from low efficiency due to bidirectional attention. Causal attention AR models support KV caching for faster inference, but often degrade in quality on long videos due to memory challenges during long-video training. In addition, beyond static prompt-based generation, interactive capabilities, such as streaming prompt inputs, are critical for dynamic content creation, enabling users to guide narratives in real time. This interactive requirement significantly increases complexity, especially in ensuring visual consistency and semantic coherence during prompt transitions. To address these challenges, LongLive adopts a causal, frame-level AR design that integrates a KV-recache mechanism that refreshes cached states with new prompts for smooth, adherent switches; streaming long tuning to enable long video training and to align training and inference (train-long-test-long); and short window attention paired with a frame-level attention sink, shorten as frame sink, preserving long-range consistency while enabling faster generation. With these key designs, LongLive fine-tunes a 1.3B-parameter short-clip model to minute-long generation in just 32 GPU-days. At inference, LongLive sustains 20.7 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100, achieves strong performance on VBench in both short and long videos. LongLive supports up to 240-second videos on a single H100 GPU. LongLive further supports INT8-quantized inference with only marginal quality loss.

keyInsights: Causal autoregressive models can achieve high-quality, long video generation and real-time interaction when enhanced with mechanisms to manage long-range consistency and dynamic prompt changes.

Recomputing the KV cache, incorporating both visual context from generated frames and new prompt information, is effective for seamless and semantically consistent prompt switching in interactive AR video generation.

A 'streaming long tuning' paradigm, where models are trained on progressively generated long sequences with detached gradients, enables efficient 'train-long-test-long' alignment without requiring massive end-to-end memory or new long-video datasets.

Combining short window attention with a 'frame sink' (persistent global tokens) is a potent strategy to balance computational efficiency with the preservation of long-range temporal consistency in attention-based models.
originalProblem: Existing diffusion models generate high-quality short videos but are computationally expensive and slow for long sequences due to quadratic memory and time complexity.

Autoregressive models struggle with quality degradation and consistency for long videos due to the 'train-short-test-long' problem and handle interactive prompt switching poorly, leading to visual discontinuities or prompt inertia.
There is a need for video generation systems that can combine real-time efficiency with high visual quality and interactive control for extended durations.
results: LONGLIVE achieves 20.7 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU, enabling real-time generation of videos up to 240 seconds, and shows a 41x speedup over SkyReels-V2.

The framework demonstrates state-of-the-art visual quality, temporal consistency, and prompt compliance for both single-prompt (30-second) and interactive multi-prompt (60-second) videos on VBench-Long metrics.
Fine-tuning a 1.3B-parameter model takes only 32 GPU-days with LoRA, and INT8 quantization further improves throughput by 1.3x and reduces model size from 2.7 GB to 1.4 GB with minimal quality loss.

Ablation studies confirm KV-recache's superiority for prompt adherence and visual continuity, and the frame sink allows short window attention to achieve consistency comparable to much larger windows while reducing compute by 28% and peak memory by 17%.
solution: Developed a causal, frame-level autoregressive (AR) framework, fine-tuned from a 1.3B-parameter short-clip model, leveraging Key-Value (KV) caching for efficient inference.

Introduced KV-Recache for interactive prompt switching, which recomputes the KV cache at prompt boundaries using the generated video prefix and new prompt to ensure smooth transitions and semantic adherence.

Implemented 'streaming long tuning' that iteratively generates and supervises short video clips conditioned on previous frames and the KV cache, using LoRA to efficiently achieve 'train-long-test-long' alignment without prohibitive memory costs.

Employed short window attention combined with a novel frame sink mechanism, where initial frames act as global sink tokens, to maintain long-range consistency and identity while drastically reducing computational cost and memory.
paper_summary.summary: LONGLIVE is a framework enabling real-time interactive long video generation, achieving 20.7 FPS on a single H100 GPU for videos up to 240 seconds. It integrates a causal autoregressive design with novel techniques like KV-Recache for smooth prompt switching and a frame sink for maintaining long-range consistency with efficient short-window attention.

title: LongLive: Real-time Interactive Long Video Generation

abstract: We present StreamBridge, a simple yet effective framework that seamlessly transforms offline Video-LLMs into streaming-capable models. It addresses two fundamental challenges in adapting existing models into online scenarios: (1) limited capability for multi-turn real-time understanding, and (2) lack of proactive response mechanisms. Specifically, StreamBridge incorporates (1) a memory buffer combined with a round-decayed compression strategy, supporting long-context multi-turn interactions, and (2) a decoupled, lightweight activation model that can be effortlessly integrated into existing Video-LLMs, enabling continuous proactive responses. To further support StreamBridge, we construct Stream-IT, a large-scale dataset tailored for streaming video understanding, featuring interleaved video-text sequences and diverse instruction formats. Extensive experiments show that StreamBridge significantly improves the streaming understanding capabilities of offline Video-LLMs across various tasks, outperforming even proprietary models such as GPT-4o and Gemini 1.5 Pro. Simultaneously, it achieves competitive or superior performance on standard video understanding benchmarks.

keyInsights: Existing powerful offline Video-LLMs can be efficiently transformed into streaming-capable models by augmenting them with modular components, preserving their core capabilities without extensive retraining.
Decoupling the proactive response mechanism via a dedicated activation model allows the main LLM to focus on understanding and generation, leading to more robust and timely autonomous behaviors.

Intelligent, round-based compression of historical visual context is critical for managing memory and maintaining low inference latency in long-duration streaming, ensuring efficient resource utilization.

Specialized instruction tuning datasets featuring interleaved video-text and proactive response patterns are essential for training models to effectively handle the complexities of real-time, multi-turn streaming interaction.
originalProblem: Offline Video-LLMs are not suited for multi-turn real-time understanding due to their inability to maintain continuous visual and conversational context across interleaved video-text sequences.
Current Video-LLMs are predominantly reactive, requiring explicit user queries and lacking the capability for human-like proactive responses in dynamic streaming environments.

Existing approaches to streaming video understanding often involve training models from scratch or tightly coupling proactive mechanisms, which can compromise general understanding or create optimization conflicts.
results: StreamBridge-equipped Qwen2-VL, fine-tuned on Stream-IT, achieved average scores of 71.30 on OVO-Bench and 77.04 on Streaming-Bench, outperforming proprietary models such as GPT-4o (64.46 and 73.28) and Gemini 1.5 Pro (69.32 and 75.69).

The framework maintained or improved performance on general video understanding tasks; for example, Oryx-1.5-7B‡ increased its VideoMME score by 6.7 to 65.5, demonstrating non-degradability and generality.

The decoupled activation model showed superior proactive capabilities on the ET-Bench, outperforming alternative streaming models in tasks like Dense Video Captioning and Step Localization and Captioning, indicated by higher text similarity scores.

Ablation studies confirmed the Round-Decayed Compression Strategy significantly reduced inference latency and prevented Out-Of-Memory errors for long video streams, while the Stream-IT dataset was crucial for improving both streaming and offline benchmarks.
solution: Introduces a Memory Buffer to store sequential visual and textual embeddings, allowing the Large Language Model to access a complete, temporally continuous history for multi-turn interactions.
Implements a Round-Decayed Compression Strategy to efficiently manage long-context memory by pruning redundant visual tokens from older dialogue rounds while preserving the most recent context.
Deploys a Plug-and-play Activation Model, a decoupled lightweight MLLM, that operates in parallel to autonomously decide when the main LLM should generate a proactive response.
Constructs Stream-IT, a large-scale, multi-turn, interleaved video-text dataset, including synthetic long-form QA, for instruction tuning to adapt models to streaming behaviors.
paper_summary.summary: StreamBridge is a framework designed to adapt existing offline Video-Large Language Models for streaming applications, enabling continuous multi-turn interaction and proactive responses. It integrates a memory buffer, a round-decayed compression strategy, and a plug-and-play activation model, achieving higher performance than proprietary models like GPT-4o on streaming benchmarks while maintaining general video understanding.

title: StreamBridge: Turning Your Offline Video Large Language Model into a Proactive Streaming Assistant

abstract: Understanding continuous video streams plays a fundamental role in real-time applications including embodied AI and autonomous driving. Unlike offline video understanding, streaming video understanding requires the ability to process video streams frame by frame, preserve historical information, and make low-latency decisions. To address these challenges, our main contributions are three-fold. (i) We develop a novel streaming video backbone, termed as StreamFormer, by incorporating causal temporal attention into a pre-trained vision transformer. This enables efficient streaming video processing while maintaining image representation capability. (ii) To train StreamFormer, we propose to unify diverse spatial-temporal video understanding tasks within a multitask visual-language alignment framework. Hence, StreamFormer learns global semantics, temporal dynamics, and fine-grained spatial relationships simultaneously. (iii) We conduct extensive experiments on online action detection, online video instance segmentation, and video question answering. StreamFormer achieves competitive results while maintaining efficiency, demonstrating its potential for real-time applications.

keyInsights: Causal temporal attention, combined with KV-Cache, maintains constant low latency and GPU memory usage for arbitrarily long video streams, which is crucial for scalable real-time processing.

The unified multitask visual-language alignment framework enables the model to learn a comprehensive understanding of video content, integrating global semantics, temporal dynamics, and fine-grained spatial relationships simultaneously.

High-quality, human-annotated datasets, even at a moderate scale (approximately 1M samples), are more effective for learning robust video representations than much larger, potentially noisy, web-scraped datasets, highlighting the value of data quality over sheer quantity.
originalProblem: Traditional "offline" video understanding models rely on bi-directional attention and process entire clips, which is unsuitable for real-time, low-latency streaming applications.
Existing streaming approaches often lose fine-grained spatial and temporal details when aggregating coarse historical information, limiting performance on tasks requiring precise understanding.
There is a need for efficient, continuous frame-by-frame processing and effective long-term context preservation under strict memory and computational constraints.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art results for online action detection, with a per-frame mean Average Precision (mAP) of 68.4 on THUMOS-14 without using optical flow features, outperforming the SigLIP baseline by 9.5 mAP.
Outperformed baseline approaches on online video instance segmentation on YoutubeVIS-2019, achieving 45.1 AP when integrated with the CTVIS framework and without COCO pre-training.
Demonstrated superior performance on video question answering benchmarks, achieving 45.0 on VideoMME and 48.4 on MLVU among models using a 224x224 input resolution.
solution: Developed StreamFormer, a specialized streaming video backbone built upon a pre-trained SigLIP Vision Transformer, incorporating causal temporal attention for sequential processing and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for efficient spatial adaptation.

Proposed a unified multitask visual-language alignment framework that reformulates diverse streaming video understanding tasks (global, temporal, spatial) into a single visual-language alignment problem.

Implemented a hierarchical training strategy leveraging six complementary objectives across global, temporal, and spatial granularities, utilizing approximately 1 million carefully curated human-annotated video datasets.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Shanghai AI Laboratory introduce StreamFormer, a streaming video backbone with causal temporal attention and a unified multitask visual-language alignment framework. This approach enables efficient, real-time video understanding, achieving 68.4 mAP on THUMOS-14 for online action detection without optical flow and outperforming baselines on online video instance segmentation and video question answering.

title: Learning Streaming Video Representation via Multitask Training

abstract: Online Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) play a critical role in supporting responsive, real-time interaction. Existing methods focus on streaming perception, lacking a synchronized logical reasoning stream. However, directly applying test-time scaling methods incurs unacceptable response latency. To address this trade-off, we propose Video Streaming Thinking (VST), a novel paradigm for streaming video understanding. It supports a thinking while watching mechanism, which activates reasoning over incoming video clips during streaming. This design improves timely comprehension and coherent cognition while preserving real-time responsiveness by amortizing LLM reasoning latency over video playback. Furthermore, we introduce a comprehensive post-training pipeline that integrates VST-SFT, which structurally adapts the offline VideoLLM to causal streaming reasoning, and VST-RL, which provides end-to-end improvement through self-exploration in a multi-turn video interaction environment. Additionally, we devise an automated training-data synthesis pipeline that uses video knowledge graphs to generate high-quality streaming QA pairs, with an entity-relation grounded streaming Chain-of-Thought to enforce multi-evidence reasoning and sustained attention to the video stream. Extensive evaluations show that VST-7B performs strongly on online benchmarks, e.g. 79.5% on StreamingBench and 59.3% on OVO-Bench. Meanwhile, VST remains competitive on offline long-form or reasoning benchmarks. Compared with Video-R1, VST responds 15.7 times faster and achieves +5.4% improvement on VideoHolmes, demonstrating higher efficiency and strong generalization across diverse video understanding tasks. Code, data, and models will be released at this https URL.

keyInsights: Proactive generation of intermediate "streaming thoughts" before a user query amortizes reasoning costs, enabling complex logical deliberation without incurring additional query-response latency.
Integrating a dual-memory system (short-term visual, long-term textual) allows VideoLLMs to maintain a coherent internal state and reason over historical context in streaming videos.

Specialized training techniques, including a streaming video attention mask and an automated data synthesis pipeline grounded in knowledge graphs, are critical for developing robust online reasoning capabilities.
originalProblem: Current online VideoLLMs efficiently process streaming inputs but lack synchronized, explicit logical reasoning capabilities.
Applying complex reasoning methods like Chain-of-Thought directly to online scenarios introduces unacceptable query-answering latency.
There is a fundamental trade-off between achieving in-depth logical reasoning and maintaining real-time responsiveness in video understanding.
results: VST-7B achieved an accuracy of 79.5% on StreamingBench, outperforming the previous open-source state-of-the-art by 2.2 percentage points and proprietary models like GPT-4o by 6.2 percentage points.

On the OVO-Bench, VST-7B scored 59.3%, exceeding the SOTA open-source streaming model Streamo by 1.4 percentage points, and showed a 4.7 percentage point improvement in Backward Tracing over Streamforest.

The model demonstrated a significantly lower query-answering latency of 0.56 seconds on VideoHolmes, a substantial reduction compared to CoT-enabled offline models which exhibited latencies up to 8.80 seconds.
solution: A "Video Streaming Thinking" (VST) paradigm that utilizes pre-query streaming intervals for proactive, autoregressive generation of "streaming thoughts" to build long-term memory.
A two-stage post-training pipeline, VST-SFT and VST-RL, which adapts an offline VideoLLM to temporal causality and refines its online reasoning capabilities.
An automated, knowledge-graph-based data synthesis pipeline to generate high-quality, diverse streaming reasoning samples, addressing data scarcity.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Huazhong University of Science and Technology and Xiaomi Inc. introduced Video Streaming Thinking (VST), a paradigm enabling Video Large Language Models to simultaneously process live video and perform logical reasoning. The VST framework achieved an accuracy of 79.5% on StreamingBench, surpassing previous open-source state-of-the-art models and some proprietary models like GPT-4o, while maintaining low query-answering latency.

title: Video Streaming Thinking: VideoLLMs Can Watch and Think Simultaneously

abstract: Benefiting from the advances in large language models and cross-modal alignment, existing multimodal large language models have achieved prominent performance in image and short video understanding. However, the understanding of long videos is still challenging, as their long-context nature results in significant computational and memory overhead. Most existing work treats long videos in the same way as short videos, which is inefficient for real-world applications and hard to generalize to even longer videos. To address these issues, we propose Flash-VStream, an efficient video language model capable of processing extremely long videos and responding to user queries in real time. Particularly, we design a Flash Memory module, containing a low-capacity context memory to aggregate long-context temporal information and model the distribution of information density, and a high-capacity augmentation memory to retrieve detailed spatial information based on this distribution. Compared to existing models, Flash-VStream achieves significant reductions in inference latency. Extensive experiments on long video benchmarks and comprehensive video benchmarks, i.e., EgoSchema, MLVU, LVBench, MVBench and Video-MME, demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance and outstanding efficiency of our method. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decoupling continuous background visual processing from on-demand query handling is essential for achieving real-time responsiveness in video understanding, independent of video length.
Long videos contain significant temporal redundancy, which can be efficiently leveraged by a dual-memory system that aggregates broad context while selectively retaining fine-grained details.
Intelligent memory management, such as K-means clustering for context synopsis and feature-centric retrieval for detailed frames, significantly improves efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.
originalProblem: Existing MLLMs struggle with prohibitive computational and memory overheads when processing extremely long video streams.
Achieving real-time, interactive responses (within one second) for free-form queries on long videos remains a significant challenge for current VLMs.
Current video language models often fail to efficiently exploit the inherent temporal redundancy in videos, leading to uniform and resource-intensive processing.
results: Flash-VStream achieves real-time query responses, delivering the first token within one second, and maintains lower, more stable GPU memory usage compared to previous online VLM approaches.
The model establishes new state-of-the-art accuracy on challenging long video understanding benchmarks, including EgoSchema (68.2%, a 4.2% improvement) and MLVU.
Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of both the Context Synopsis Memory (CSM) and Detail Augmentation Memory (DAM), demonstrating their synergistic contribution to overall performance.
solution: A two-process asynchronous framework separates continuous visual frame processing (Frame Handler) from real-time query answering (Question Handler), preventing computational bottlenecks.

A novel dual-memory module, Flash Memory, compresses long video content into a fixed-size representation using a Context Synopsis Memory (CSM) for temporal context and a Detail Augmentation Memory (DAM) for critical spatial details.
Adaptive Multimodal Rotary Position Embedding (AM-RoPE) handles the flexible and non-uniform arrangement of varying-resolution and context-aggregated visual tokens for the language model.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University and ByteDance Inc. developed Flash-VStream, a system for real-time understanding of long video streams that separates visual processing from language inference. It utilizes a novel dual-memory module to efficiently manage temporal context and fine-grained details, enabling sub-second response times for user queries and establishing new state-of-the-art accuracy on various long video benchmarks.

title: Flash-VStream: Efficient Real-Time Understanding for Long Video Streams

abstract: Recent advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated significant improvement in offline video understanding. However, extending these capabilities to streaming video inputs, remains challenging, as existing models struggle to simultaneously maintain stable understanding performance, real-time responses, and low GPU memory overhead. To address this challenge, we propose HERMES, a novel training-free architecture for real-time and accurate understanding of video streams. Based on a mechanistic attention investigation, we conceptualize KV cache as a hierarchical memory framework that encapsulates video information across multiple granularities. During inference, HERMES reuses a compact KV cache, enabling efficient streaming understanding under resource constraints. Notably, HERMES requires no auxiliary computations upon the arrival of user queries, thereby guaranteeing real-time responses for continuous video stream interactions, which achieves 10$\times$ faster TTFT compared to prior SOTA. Even when reducing video tokens by up to 68% compared with uniform sampling, HERMES achieves superior or comparable accuracy across all benchmarks, with up to 11.4% gains on streaming datasets.

keyInsights: MLLM decoder layers exhibit distinct attention patterns: shallow layers show recency bias (sensory memory), deep layers focus on sparse, rhythmic anchor tokens (long-term memory), and middle layers act as transitional working memory.
Efficient and interpretable KV cache management can be achieved by aligning token eviction strategies with these observed layer-wise attention behaviors.
Aggregating evicted tokens into 'summary tokens' in deep layers effectively preserves long-term semantic context without increasing memory footprint.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle to achieve stable performance, real-time responses, and low GPU memory overhead when processing continuous streaming video inputs.
Existing external memory methods for long video contexts introduce high latency due to retrieval operations and often require costly model-specific training.
Prior internal memory (KV cache) methods for streaming often offload video segments to external storage, requiring additional retrieval steps and incurring latency at query time.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art accuracy on streaming video benchmarks, with a 6.13% gain on StreamingBench (Real-Time) and 6.93% on OVO-Bench (Average) over the base Qwen2.5-VL-7B model.
Maintains an exceptionally low Time to First Token (TTFT) of less than 30 ms, representing a 10x speedup compared to prior training-free methods.
Demonstrates a stable and compact GPU memory footprint, independent of the number of input frames, and reduces peak memory by 1.04x compared to LiveVLM under 256 frames.
solution: A training-free and plug-and-play framework re-conceptualizes the KV cache as a hierarchical memory system, mapping MLLM decoder layers to sensory, working, and long-term memory.
It employs layer-specific token retention strategies for the KV cache, guided by mechanistic attention investigation.
Integrates cross-layer memory smoothing to maintain consistency across layers and specialized position re-indexing strategies for continuous streaming.
paper_summary.summary: HERMES introduces a training-free framework that re-conceptualizes the KV cache as a hierarchical memory system for efficient streaming video understanding. This approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on streaming benchmarks while maintaining stable low latency (under 30 ms TTFT) and a compact, constant GPU memory footprint.

title: HERMES: KV Cache as Hierarchical Memory for Efficient Streaming Video Understanding

abstract:
Recent advances in Large Multi-modal Models (LMMs) are primarily focused on
offline video understanding. Instead, streaming video understanding poses great
challenges to recent models due to its time-sensitive, omni-modal and
interactive characteristics. In this work, we aim to extend the streaming video
understanding from a new perspective and propose a novel task named Visual
Instruction Feedback in which models should be aware of visual contents and
learn to extract instructions from them. For example, when users wave their
hands to agents, agents should recognize the gesture and start conversations
with welcome information. Thus, following instructions in visual modality
greatly enhances user-agent interactions. To facilitate research, we define
seven key subtasks highly relevant to visual modality and collect the
ViSpeak-Instruct dataset for training and the ViSpeak-Bench for evaluation.
Further, we propose the ViSpeak model, which is a SOTA streaming video
understanding LMM with GPT-4o-level performance on various streaming video
understanding benchmarks. After finetuning on our ViSpeak-Instruct dataset,
ViSpeak is equipped with basic visual instruction feedback ability, serving as
a solid baseline for future research.

keyInsights: Real-time visual instruction feedback in streaming video is a distinct challenge from offline video understanding, requiring immediate analysis of continuous multi-modal inputs.

A specialized LMM architecture featuring multi-modal encoders, a two-stream chat template for interruptions, and an 'informative head' for response timing is effective for visual instruction feedback.
A three-stage finetuning recipe, progressing from template alignment to streaming QA and then visual instruction feedback, successfully adapts models for real-time interaction capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing Large Multi-modal Models (LMMs) primarily focus on offline video analysis, where the entire video content is available in advance.
Streaming video applications require real-time processing and immediate responses to continuously arriving content, which current LMMs do not adequately address.
There was a lack of a defined task and benchmark for AI systems to understand and respond to visual cues or instructions within live streaming video interactions.
results: ViSpeak achieves state-of-the-art performance on streaming video understanding benchmarks and is comparable to GPT-4o in omni-source understanding.
The model significantly outperforms existing open-source models on the introduced Visual Instruction Feedback tasks, demonstrating robustness across various visual instructions.
Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of architectural components, with the two-stream chat template improving interruption handling and the informative head enhancing response timing accuracy.
solution: The paper introduces a new task called 'Visual Instruction Feedback' for AI systems to respond to visual cues in streaming videos, decomposing it into seven subtasks (e.g., Visual Wake-Up, Gesture Understanding).

It proposes ViSpeak, an omni-modal LMM with multi-modal encoders, a two-stream chat template for continuous interaction, an informative head for timely responses, and a Large Language Model for generation.
Researchers created two datasets, ViSpeak-Bench (1,000 videos for evaluation) and ViSpeak-Instruct (34,000 samples for training), and developed a three-stage finetuning process for the model.
paper_summary.summary: ViSpeak introduces the 'Visual Instruction Feedback' task, enabling large multi-modal models to understand and respond to visual cues in real-time streaming videos. The ViSpeak model achieves state-of-the-art performance on streaming video understanding and outperforms existing open-source models on this new task.

title: ViSpeak: Visual Instruction Feedback in Streaming Videos

abstract:
This paper introduces VideoScan, an efficient vision-language model (VLM)
inference framework designed for real-time video interaction that effectively
comprehends and retains streamed video inputs while delivering rapid and
accurate responses. A longstanding challenge in video
understanding--particularly for long-term or real-time applications--stems from
the substantial computational overhead caused by the extensive length of visual
tokens. To address this, VideoScan employs a single semantic carrier token to
represent each frame, progressively reducing computational and memory overhead
during its two-phase inference process: prefilling and decoding. The embedding
of the semantic carrier token is derived from an optimized aggregation of
frame-level visual features, ensuring compact yet semantically rich
representations. Critically, the corresponding key-value pairs are trained to
retain contextual semantics from prior frames, enabling efficient memory
management without sacrificing temporal coherence. During inference, the visual
tokens of each frame are processed only once during the prefilling phase and
subsequently discarded in the decoding stage, eliminating redundant
computations. This design ensures efficient VLM inference even under stringent
real-time constraints. Comprehensive experiments on diverse offline and online
benchmarks demonstrate that LLaVA-Video, supported by our method, achieves up
to $\sim 5\times$ and $1.29\times$ speedups compared to its original version
and previous efficient streaming video understanding approaches, respectively.
Crucially, these improvements are attained while maintaining competitive
performance and ensuring stable GPU memory consumption (consistently $\sim
18$GB, independent of video duration).

keyInsights: Transformer decoders exhibit a "semantic flow" where deeper layers progressively aggregate and refine contextual coherence from preceding tokens, facilitating inherent "in-context summarization."

This observed semantic flow enables effective extreme visual token reduction to one semantic carrier token per frame, as Large Language Models (LLMs) can derive knowledge from these highly compressed representations.
Strategic training with a semantic-aware causal mask can reinforce the encapsulation of visual information within these carrier tokens and improve temporal reasoning.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face high computational and memory overhead when processing long, real-time video streams due to the extensive length of visual tokens per frame.

Current token reduction strategies are either insufficient (modest reduction rates) or instruction-dependent, limiting their general applicability and reusability for dynamic streaming video understanding.
Real-time streaming video understanding frameworks struggle with unstable GPU memory consumption that increases with video duration, making long-term interactions unfeasible.
results: Achieved competitive accuracy on video understanding benchmarks (e.g., MVBench, MLVU), retaining over 85% of LLaVA-Video's accuracy with over 99% reduction in vision token overhead.
Demonstrated a 5x speedup in serving rate (6 Frames Per Second) compared to LLaVA-Video and approximately 50% less VRAM consumption (18GB vs 36GB) than Flash-VStream-7B/ReKV in real-time scenarios.
Maintained stable GPU memory consumption, consistently around 18GB, regardless of video duration, effectively enabling long-term streaming video understanding.
solution: Proposes using a single "semantic carrier token" per video frame, derived from average pooling and designed to propagate contextual information via reusable Key-Value (KV) pairs.

Implements a two-phase inference process (prefilling and decoding) where original high-dimensional visual tokens are discarded after the initial pass, and decoding relies solely on cached semantic carrier KV pairs.
Integrates a dynamic memory mechanism with a fixed-capacity bank and a feature duplication-based eviction strategy to maintain stable GPU memory consumption throughout long video streams.
paper_summary.summary: VideoScan introduces an inference framework for streaming video understanding, representing each frame with a single "semantic carrier token" to achieve over 99% vision token reduction. This approach enables 5x faster processing than LLaVA-Video and reduces VRAM consumption by 50% compared to ReKV, while maintaining competitive accuracy and stable GPU memory for long-duration interactions.

title: VideoScan: Enabling Efficient Streaming Video Understanding via  Frame-level Semantic Carriers

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made significant progress in visual-language reasoning, but their ability to efficiently handle long videos remains limited. Despite recent advances in long-context MLLMs, storing and attending to the key-value (KV) cache for long visual contexts incurs substantial memory and computational overhead. Existing visual compression methods require either encoding the entire visual context before compression or having access to the questions in advance, which is impractical for long video understanding and multi-turn conversational settings. In this work, we propose StreamMem, a query-agnostic KV cache memory mechanism for streaming video understanding. Specifically, StreamMem encodes new video frames in a streaming manner, compressing the KV cache using attention scores between visual tokens and generic query tokens, while maintaining a fixed-size KV memory to enable efficient question answering (QA) in memory-constrained, long-video scenarios. Evaluation on three long video understanding and two streaming video question answering benchmarks shows that StreamMem achieves state-of-the-art performance in query-agnostic KV cache compression and is competitive with query-aware compression approaches.

keyInsights: Auxiliary chat template tokens can serve as an effective query-agnostic proxy to identify salient visual information, enabling robust KV cache compression without requiring explicit user questions.

Combining attention-based pruning with weighted frame-wise KV merging effectively condenses information, retaining both fine-grained salient tokens and global frame context, which is crucial for long-range video understanding.

A carefully designed, fixed-size KV memory, even if smaller than the full uncompressed cache, can capture more relevant information and achieve superior performance by intelligently discarding redundant data.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) face prohibitive memory and computational overhead when processing long videos due to the large Key-Value (KV) caches of visual tokens, leading to Out-Of-Memory errors.
Existing visual compression methods are often query-aware, requiring prior knowledge of user questions, which is impractical for real-time streaming or multi-turn conversational scenarios.
Previous streaming video solutions either store all KV cache (leading to unbounded memory growth) or use simplistic eviction strategies that lose valuable historical information over time.
results: StreamMem consistently achieved state-of-the-art performance in query-agnostic KV cache compression on MLVU, EgoSchema, and VideoMME benchmarks, outperforming baselines like LiveVLM and InfiniPot-V for various MLLMs.
With a 24K token budget, StreamMem surpassed the performance of full KV storage on the MLVU benchmark using Qwen2VL-7B, demonstrating its efficiency in retaining highly salient information.

Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of each component: chat template queries performed comparably to generic text queries, weighted merging was superior to no merging, and input frame filtering consistently improved performance.
solution: StreamMem employs lightweight input frame filtering to remove temporal redundancy by merging highly similar consecutive frames before MLLM processing.

A novel query-agnostic KV cache compression mechanism utilizes auxiliary chat template tokens to estimate visual token saliency, enabling attention-based pruning and weighted frame-wise KV merging to maintain a fixed memory budget.
The YaRN context window extension technique is integrated to extend visual context capacity and preserve positional consistency for compressed tokens, improving long video understanding.
paper_summary.summary: StreamMem introduces a training-free, query-agnostic framework for multimodal large language models to efficiently process long streaming videos by maintaining a fixed-size Key-Value cache memory. This method significantly reduces memory overhead and improves performance on long-video question answering benchmarks, often surpassing full KV storage settings with smaller memory budgets.

title: StreamMem: Query-Agnostic KV Cache Memory for Streaming Video Understanding

abstract: With the success of large language models (LLMs), integrating the vision model into LLMs to build vision-language foundation models has gained much more interest recently. However, existing LLM-based large multimodal models (e.g., Video-LLaMA, VideoChat) can only take in a limited number of frames for short video understanding. In this study, we mainly focus on designing an efficient and effective model for long-term video understanding. Instead of trying to process more frames simultaneously like most existing work, we propose to process videos in an online manner and store past video information in a memory bank. This allows our model to reference historical video content for long-term analysis without exceeding LLMs' context length constraints or GPU memory limits. Our memory bank can be seamlessly integrated into current multimodal LLMs in an off-the-shelf manner. We conduct extensive experiments on various video understanding tasks, such as long-video understanding, video question answering, and video captioning, and our model can achieve state-of-the-art performances across multiple datasets. Code available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Online processing with a dual memory bank (visual and query) effectively addresses the challenges of long-term video understanding by retaining historical context.
The proposed Memory Bank Compression (MBC) method intelligently reduces memory bank size by merging redundant tokens while preserving crucial temporal information.
The memory-augmented module can be integrated "off-the-shelf" into existing LMMs (e.g., InstructBLIP) without extensive retraining, enabling plug-and-play long-term video capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) are primarily designed for short video understanding or single images, leading to degraded performance or computational infeasibility for long video sequences.
Large Language Models (LLMs), which form the backbone of LMMs, have finite context windows, which are quickly exceeded when concatenating visual features from many video frames.
Directly processing numerous high-resolution video frames simultaneously demands prohibitive GPU memory, making long-term video analysis impractical.
results: Achieved 3.8% average top-1 accuracy improvement over the previous state-of-the-art S5 model on the LVU benchmark for long-term video classification, and surpassed S5 by 2.3% on Breakfast and 2.4% on COIN.

Established new state-of-the-art performance on MSRVTT-QA and MSVD-QA, outperforming Video-LLaMA on all three VQA datasets (MSRVTT, MSVD, ActivityNet) and consistently ranking among the top two for video captioning on MSRVTT, MSVD, and YouCook2.

Ablation studies confirmed that both visual and query memory banks are critical, leading to substantial performance boosts (e.g., 14.7% on LVU), and Memory Bank Compression (MBC) outperformed FIFO by 1.7-4.5% in accuracy, demonstrating its effectiveness in retaining information while compressing.
solution: Introduces MA-LMM, a Memory-Augmented Large Multimodal Model that processes videos in an online (auto-regressive) manner.
Integrates dual memory banks (visual and query) into the Q-Former to store and retrieve past video information, enabling the model to access long-term context.

Employs a novel Memory Bank Compression (MBC) technique to manage memory bank growth by dynamically merging similar adjacent tokens, ensuring efficient memory usage without losing critical temporal details.
paper_summary.summary: A Memory-Augmented Large Multimodal Model (MA-LMM) is introduced for efficient online long-term video understanding, addressing the context length and GPU memory constraints of existing LMMs. The model achieves state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks, including a 3.8% improvement in average top-1 accuracy on the LVU benchmark for long-term video classification.

title: MA-LMM: Memory-Augmented Large Multimodal Model for Long-Term Video Understanding

abstract: Active Real-time interaction with video LLMs introduces a new paradigm for human-computer interaction, where the model not only understands user intent but also responds while continuously processing streaming video on the fly. Unlike offline video LLMs, which analyze the entire video before answering questions, active real-time interaction requires three capabilities: 1) Perception: real-time video monitoring and interaction capturing. 2) Decision: raising proactive interaction in proper situations, 3) Reaction: continuous interaction with users. However, inherent conflicts exist among the desired capabilities. The Decision and Reaction require a contrary Perception scale and grain, and the autoregressive decoding blocks the real-time Perception and Decision during the Reaction. To unify the conflicted capabilities within a harmonious system, we present Dispider, a system that disentangles Perception, Decision, and Reaction. Dispider features a lightweight proactive streaming video processing module that tracks the video stream and identifies optimal moments for interaction. Once the interaction is triggered, an asynchronous interaction module provides detailed responses, while the processing module continues to monitor the video in the meantime. Our disentangled and asynchronous design ensures timely, contextually accurate, and computationally efficient responses, making Dispider ideal for active real-time interaction for long-duration video streams. Experiments show that Dispider not only maintains strong performance in conventional video QA tasks, but also significantly surpasses previous online models in streaming scenario responses, thereby validating the effectiveness of our architecture. The code and model are released at \url{this https URL}.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Disentangling capabilities allows parallel processing of video stream and response generation
Scene-based segmentation preserves structural video information better than uniform segmentation
Decision module can independently determine when responses are needed without waiting for reaction module
Asynchronous design enables continuous video monitoring during response generation
originalProblem: Traditional video LLMs can only analyze videos after they finish playing
Current streaming approaches like VideoLLM-online block video processing during response generation
Inherent conflicts exist between real-time perception and response generation capabilities
results: Outperforms VideoLLM-online on streaming benchmarks, particularly in proactive output tasks
Achieves competitive performance on conventional video QA benchmarks
Shows superior temporal grounding capabilities compared to specialized temporal models
Demonstrates effective handling of both short and long-duration video content
solution: Introduces Dispider with three disentangled modules: perception, decision, and reaction
Uses scene-based video segmentation for efficient processing of streaming content
Implements asynchronous interaction module that generates responses without blocking video processing
Employs special tokens and memory mechanisms to track interaction context
paper_summary.summary: This paper introduces a disentangled framework called Dispider that enables real-time interaction with video Large Language Models by separating perception, decision, and reaction capabilities

title: Dispider: Enabling Video LLMs with Active Real-Time Interaction via Disentangled Perception, Decision, and Reaction

abstract:
Recent Large Language Models have been enhanced with vision capabilities,
enabling them to comprehend images, videos, and interleaved vision-language
content. However, the learning methods of these large multimodal models
typically treat videos as predetermined clips, making them less effective and
efficient at handling streaming video inputs. In this paper, we propose a novel
Learning-In-Video-Stream (LIVE) framework, which enables temporally aligned,
long-context, and real-time conversation within a continuous video stream. Our
LIVE framework comprises comprehensive approaches to achieve video streaming
dialogue, encompassing: (1) a training objective designed to perform language
modeling for continuous streaming inputs, (2) a data generation scheme that
converts offline temporal annotations into a streaming dialogue format, and (3)
an optimized inference pipeline to speed up the model responses in real-world
video streams. With our LIVE framework, we built VideoLLM-online model upon
Llama-2/Llama-3 and demonstrate its significant advantages in processing
streaming videos. For instance, on average, our model can support streaming
dialogue in a 5-minute video clip at over 10 FPS on an A100 GPU. Moreover, it
also showcases state-of-the-art performance on public offline video benchmarks,
such as recognition, captioning, and forecasting. The code, model, data, and
demo have been made available at https://showlab.github.io/videollm-online.

keyInsights: Introducing a Streaming End-of-Sequence (EOS) prediction objective enables the model to intelligently decide when to respond and when to remain silent based on incoming video frames.
A streaming dialogue generation scheme converts offline temporal annotations into online dialogues, addressing data scarcity for real-time video conversations.
An optimized inference pipeline with probability correction, continuous Key-Value cache, and parallelized visual encoding/language decoding achieves real-time processing speeds.
originalProblem: Existing Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) primarily process videos as pre-determined clips, making them unsuitable for real-time interaction with continuous video streams.
Challenges in streaming video processing include providing temporally aligned responses, handling long visual and linguistic contexts, and achieving real-time answer generation.
Current visual dialogue systems typically rely on static images or single video clips, lacking adaptability to continuous visual input, while online video understanding systems are task-specific.
results: VideoLLM-online achieves processing speeds of 10-15 FPS on an A100 GPU and 5-10 FPS on a 3090 GPU for streaming dialogue in a 5-minute video clip, with less than 20 GB memory cost.
The model demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on offline benchmarks for short- and long-term activity recognition and forecasting, including COIN and Ego4D LTA.
The system enables practical applications such as real-time AR assistant contextual awareness and proactive cooking guidance by understanding ongoing activities and maintaining temporal coherence.
solution: The LIVE (Learning-In-Video-Stream) framework is introduced, featuring a multimodal architecture built on a language model (Llama-2/Llama-3) with an image encoder and MLP projector.
A Streaming End-of-Sequence (EOS) prediction objective is implemented, training the model to predict EOS tokens on redundant frame tokens to manage response timing and long video contexts.
A streaming dialogue generation scheme creates online dialogues from offline temporal annotations using question templates and LLMs, enabling free-form chatting aligned with video events.
paper_summary.summary: VideoLLM-online, from researchers at the National University of Singapore and Reality Labs Research at Meta, introduces the LIVE framework for real-time processing of continuous video streams. This system allows AI assistants to provide temporally aligned, context-aware responses, achieving speeds of 10-15 frames per second on an A100 GPU.

title: VideoLLM-online: Online Video Large Language Model for Streaming Video

abstract:
Benefiting from the advancements in large language models and cross-modal
alignment, existing multi-modal video understanding methods have achieved
prominent performance in offline scenario. However, online video streams, as
one of the most common media forms in the real world, have seldom received
attention. Compared to offline videos, the 'dynamic' nature of online video
streams poses challenges for the direct application of existing models and
introduces new problems, such as the storage of extremely long-term
information, interaction between continuous visual content and 'asynchronous'
user questions. Therefore, in this paper we present Flash-VStream, a
video-language model that simulates the memory mechanism of human. Our model is
able to process extremely long video streams in real-time and respond to user
queries simultaneously. Compared to existing models, Flash-VStream achieves
significant reductions in inference latency and VRAM consumption, which is
intimately related to performing understanding of online streaming video. In
addition, given that existing video understanding benchmarks predominantly
concentrate on offline scenario, we propose VStream-QA, a novel question
answering benchmark specifically designed for online video streaming
understanding. Comparisons with popular existing methods on the proposed
benchmark demonstrate the superiority of our method for such challenging
setting. To verify the generalizability of our approach, we further evaluate it
on existing video understanding benchmarks and achieves state-of-the-art
performance in offline scenarios as well. All code, models, and datasets are
available at the https://invinciblewyq.github.io/vstream-page/

keyInsights: Mimicking human cognitive processes, specifically the parallel handling of perception/memorization and recall/answering, is effective for real-time continuous data understanding.
Efficient, multi-granular memory systems (like STAR memory) are crucial for compressing and retaining long-term visual information without incurring prohibitive computational costs.

Decoupling the continuous visual processing (frame handling) from the query-based reasoning (question handling) enables significantly reduced inference latency and VRAM consumption in real-time scenarios.
originalProblem: Existing Video-Language Models (VLMs) were designed for finite, offline videos, struggling with the continuous, dynamic nature of real-time online streams.
Processing extremely long video streams leads to prohibitive GPU memory consumption and unacceptable inference latency for existing VLM architectures.
There was a lack of benchmarks specifically designed to evaluate online, long-form video understanding with timestamped queries.
results: Flash-VStream achieved state-of-the-art performance on the VStream-QA benchmark (e.g., 57.3% accuracy on RVS-Ego), outperforming previous SoTA methods.

It demonstrated significantly reduced inference latency (maintaining sub-second response times regardless of video length) and low VRAM consumption (16.03GB for 1000 frames), making real-time processing feasible.
The model also generalized well, achieving state-of-the-art results on four conventional zero-shot offline video QA benchmarks (e.g., 80.3% Acc on MSVD-QA).
solution: Introduces Flash-VStream, a "4-step, 2-process" architecture with a continuous Frame Handler and a query-triggered Question Handler.

Develops the Spatial-Temporal-Abstract-Retrieved (STAR) Memory system, which efficiently compresses and stores visual information across different granularities (spatial, temporal, abstract, retrieved).
Proposes VStream-QA, a novel benchmark featuring timestamped questions over extremely long videos (30-60 minutes) to evaluate online video understanding.
paper_summary.summary: Flash-VStream presents a memory-based video-language model designed for real-time understanding of extremely long online video streams, alongside a new benchmark called VStream-QA. The model demonstrates enhanced accuracy on video question answering, coupled with a notable reduction in inference latency and VRAM consumption compared to prior methods.

title:
Flash-VStream: Memory-Based Real-Time Understanding for Long Video
  Streams

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently achieved remarkable progress in video understanding. However, their effectiveness in real-time streaming scenarios remains limited due to storage constraints of historical visual features and insufficient real-time spatiotemporal reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose StreamForest, a novel architecture specifically designed for streaming video understanding. Central to StreamForest is the Persistent Event Memory Forest, a memory mechanism that adaptively organizes video frames into multiple event-level tree structures. This process is guided by penalty functions based on temporal distance, content similarity, and merge frequency, enabling efficient long-term memory retention under limited computational resources. To enhance real-time perception, we introduce a Fine-grained Spatiotemporal Window, which captures detailed short-term visual cues to improve current scene perception. Additionally, we present OnlineIT, an instruction-tuning dataset tailored for streaming video tasks. OnlineIT significantly boosts MLLM performance in both real-time perception and future prediction. To evaluate generalization in practical applications, we introduce ODV-Bench, a new benchmark focused on real-time streaming video understanding in autonomous driving scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that StreamForest achieves the state-of-the-art performance, with accuracies of 77.3% on StreamingBench, 60.5% on OVBench, and 55.6% on OVO-Bench. In particular, even under extreme visual token compression (limited to 1024 tokens), the model retains 96.8% of its average accuracy in eight benchmarks relative to the default setting. These results underscore the robustness, efficiency, and generalizability of StreamForest for streaming video understanding.

keyInsights: An event-level, tree-structured memory (PEMF) with adaptive penalty-guided compression is more effective for long-term streaming context retention than frame-centric or static memory strategies, preserving semantic coherence and temporal diversity.

Integrating a fine-grained short-term perception window (FSTW) with long-term event memory (PEMF) provides complementary benefits, enabling robust real-time understanding without sacrificing long-horizon reasoning.

Specialized instruction-tuning data (OnlineIT) focused on real-time perception and future prediction, particularly in dynamic environments like autonomous driving, is crucial to mitigate hallucinations and enhance MLLM robustness to spatiotemporal distribution shifts.
originalProblem: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) primarily focus on offline video understanding, struggling with the real-time processing and memory constraints of continuous streaming video.

Efficiently retaining long-term contextual understanding in streaming scenarios while managing the prohibitive computational and storage costs of continuous high-resolution visual features is challenging.
Aggressive data compression methods often used in streaming lead to a loss of fine-grained visual details, hindering accurate real-time spatiotemporal reasoning and event perception.
results: StreamForest achieved 71.2% accuracy on the new ODV-Bench (autonomous driving), significantly outperforming existing online and offline MLLMs.

The model set new state-of-the-art performance among open-source online video MLLMs on StreamingBench (77.3%), OVBench (60.5%), and OVO-Bench (55.6%), surpassing models like Gemini 1.5 Pro on some benchmarks.

It demonstrated remarkable robustness, retaining 96.8% of its average accuracy across eight benchmarks even when compressing visual tokens by 99.8% to a strict 1K limit, while maintaining stable memory usage and lightweight inference.
solution: StreamForest combines a Fine-grained Spatiotemporal Window (FSTW) for detailed real-time perception with a Persistent Event Memory Forest (PEMF) for efficient, adaptive long-term context retention.

PEMF organizes video segments into hierarchical, tree-structured event nodes and uses a multi-penalty system (similarity, merge count, temporal distance) to adaptively compress and consolidate memory.

A dedicated instruction-tuning dataset, OnlineIT (general and autonomous driving), and a new benchmark, ODV-Bench, were created to train and evaluate MLLMs for real-time streaming tasks, particularly future prediction and risk assessment.
paper_summary.summary: StreamForest introduces a novel multimodal large language model architecture designed for efficient online video understanding, leveraging a persistent event memory and a fine-grained spatiotemporal window. The model demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on streaming video benchmarks, including a new autonomous driving benchmark, and maintains robust accuracy even under extreme visual token compression.

title: StreamForest: Efficient Online Video Understanding with Persistent Event Memory

abstract:
We introduce ProVideLLM, an end-to-end framework for real-time procedural
video understanding. ProVideLLM integrates a multimodal cache configured to
store two types of tokens - verbalized text tokens, which provide compressed
textual summaries of long-term observations, and visual tokens, encoded with
DETR-QFormer to capture fine-grained details from short-term observations. This
design reduces token count by 22x over existing methods in representing one
hour of long-term observations while effectively encoding fine-granularity of
the present. By interleaving these tokens in our multimodal cache, ProVideLLM
ensures sub-linear scaling of memory and compute with video length, enabling
per-frame streaming inference at 10 FPS and streaming dialogue at 25 FPS, with
a minimal 2GB GPU memory footprint. ProVideLLM also sets new state-of-the-art
results on six procedural tasks across four datasets.

keyInsights: Interleaving textual summaries of past video content with recent visual tokens in a cache significantly reduces memory usage while preserving both long-term context and fine-grained visual details.
Explicitly modeling hand-object interactions via a specialized DETR-QFormer improves fine-grained action recognition, which is crucial for understanding procedural steps.

Visual encoders like DINOv2, which capture higher temporal variance, are more effective for understanding dynamic procedural video content compared to models optimized primarily for static image-text alignment.
originalProblem: Existing Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) are computationally and memory intensive, making them unsuitable for real-time streaming applications like augmented reality (AR) assistants.
Procedural videos present challenges due to their long temporal dependencies, requiring models to maintain context over extended periods.
Understanding detailed steps in procedural videos necessitates capturing subtle fine-grained hand-object interactions.
results: ProVideLLM outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods across six procedural video understanding tasks on four datasets, showing improved accuracy in step recognition, next-step prediction, and goal identification.
The framework achieves 22x greater memory efficiency, using fewer tokens to represent one hour of video, and maintains nearly constant memory usage regardless of video length.
It supports real-time inference, processing frames at 10 FPS and streaming dialogue at 25 FPS, with a low memory footprint of 2GB of GPU memory.
solution: ProVideLLM introduces a framework with a DINOv2 visual encoder, a novel DETR-QFormer for hand-object interaction modeling, and a language decoder based on a pre-trained Large Language Model.
A multimodal interleaved cache verbalizes long-term video observations into textual summaries and interleaves them with recent visual tokens to maintain context while minimizing memory.

The DETR-QFormer uses specialized learnable queries to focus on hands and objects, connecting visual representations to the language space and being trained with dual language modeling and hand-object detection objectives.
paper_summary.summary: ProVideLLM introduces a memory-efficient framework for streaming procedural video understanding, which employs a multimodal interleaved cache and a DETR-QFormer to capture fine-grained hand-object interactions. The system achieves state-of-the-art performance across six procedural video tasks while maintaining real-time inference and constant memory usage for extended video lengths.

title: Memory-efficient Streaming VideoLLMs for Real-time Procedural Video  Understanding

abstract: Proactive and real-time interactive experiences are essential for human-like AI companions, yet face three key challenges: (1) achieving low-latency inference under continuous streaming inputs, (2) autonomously deciding when to respond, and (3) controlling both quality and quantity of generated content to meet real-time constraints. In this work, we instantiate AI companions through two gaming scenarios, commentator and guide, selected for their suitability for automatic evaluation. We introduce the Live Gaming Benchmark, a large-scale dataset with three representative scenarios: solo commentary, co-commentary, and user guidance, and present Proact-VL, a general framework that shapes multimodal language models into proactive, real-time interactive agents capable of human-like environment perception and interaction. Extensive experiments show Proact-VL achieves superior response latency and quality while maintaining strong video understanding capabilities, demonstrating its practicality for real-time interactive applications.

keyInsights: Discretizing continuous video into short, fixed-duration chunks combined with a persistent KV cache enables real-time responsiveness and coherent context over long dialogue histories.
Integrating a dedicated proactive decision mechanism, trained with a multi-tier loss function, allows AI companions to control speaking dynamics, mirroring human conversational turn-taking.

The Live Gaming Dataset, comprising 561 hours of expert commentary across diverse games, is crucial for training and benchmarking proactive, real-time multimodal AI companions in complex, dynamic environments.
originalProblem: Existing VideoLLMs struggle with low-latency inference and sustained interaction under continuous video streams, often optimized for offline question-answering protocols.
AI companions lack autonomous control over interaction dynamics, such as deciding when to respond and controlling response length, leading to either constant talking or delayed, lengthy answers.
Prior real-time models prioritize low latency but lack explicit speaking controls, while proactive models offer coarser temporal granularity and higher latency for full responses.
results: Proact-VL achieved the highest overall text quality (CC: 49.23, LiveU: 6.52, FinalQ: 5.03) and superior proactivity timing (F1: 64.87, TimeDiff: 1.71) across various gaming scenarios, outperforming commercial models in several aspects.

The framework demonstrated strong generalization capabilities on common (Ego4D) and unseen (Black Myth: Wukong) scenarios, and exhibited robust, stable performance over long-duration streaming evaluations (up to 50 minutes).

Ablation studies confirmed that both the transition-smoothed classification loss and stability regularization are critical components, contributing significantly to effective response behavior and maintaining generation quality.
solution: Proact-VL employs a chunk-wise input schema, processing video streams in one-second segments and generating short utterance segments, maintained by a persistent transformer KV cache.
A proactive response mechanism with a lightweight MLP head and a decision token (<|FLAG|>) autonomously determines whether and when to speak based on a learned probability threshold.

A dual-objective training strategy combines causal language modeling loss for utterance quality with a specialized response loss (transition-smoothed classification and stability regularization) to govern speaking behavior and ensure temporal consistency.
paper_summary.summary: Proact-VL, a real-time VideoLLM, empowers AI companions to proactively generate human-like commentary and guidance for continuous video streams. The framework achieves superior text quality and precise response timing in gaming scenarios, reflected by an F1 score of 64.87 and a TimeDiff of 1.71, while demonstrating robust performance in sustained streaming environments.

title: Proact-VL: A Proactive VideoLLM for Real-Time AI Companions

abstract: Real-time streaming video understanding in domains such as autonomous driving and intelligent surveillance poses challenges beyond conventional offline video processing, requiring continuous perception, proactive decision making, and responsive interaction based on dynamically evolving visual content. However, existing methods rely on alternating perception-reaction or asynchronous triggers, lacking task-driven planning and future anticipation, which limits their real-time responsiveness and proactive decision making in evolving video streams. To this end, we propose a StreamAgent that anticipates the temporal intervals and spatial regions expected to contain future task-relevant information to enable proactive and goal-driven responses. Specifically, we integrate question semantics and historical observations through prompting the anticipatory agent to anticipate the temporal progression of key events, align current observations with the expected future evidence, and subsequently adjust the perception action (e.g., attending to task-relevant regions or continuously tracking in subsequent frames). To enable efficient inference, we design a streaming KV-cache memory mechanism that constructs a hierarchical memory structure for selective recall of relevant tokens, enabling efficient semantic retrieval while reducing the overhead of storing all tokens in the traditional KV-cache. Extensive experiments on streaming and long video understanding tasks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing methods in response accuracy and real-time efficiency, highlighting its practical value for real-world streaming scenarios.

keyInsights: Proactive temporal and spatial anticipation, combined with adaptive planning, enables AI agents to defer responses until sufficient evidence is accumulated, leading to more accurate and timely decision-making.
Augmenting streaming MLLMs with external tools for active perception significantly improves performance by enabling goal-driven information gathering rather than passive observation.

Efficient memory management via a streaming KV-cache, which dynamically offloads irrelevant context and selectively recalls relevant information, is critical for scaling MLLMs to real-time, long-form video understanding tasks.
originalProblem: Existing streaming video understanding models operate reactively, lacking proactive decision-making and anticipation of future events, which is crucial for real-world applications.
Current methods struggle with inefficient response timing, often making premature or delayed decisions due to a lack of sophisticated planning or active information gathering.
Processing continuous video streams generates a "long-context bottleneck," leading to excessive memory consumption and computational delays in existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).
results: StreamAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance on streaming benchmarks, including a 10.7% improvement in "Forward Active Responding" accuracy over Dispider on OVO-Bench, and superior results on StreamingBench and OVBench.

Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the incremental Markov memory, heuristic-based planning strategy, and tool-use, demonstrating their individual contributions to accuracy and contextual understanding.

The streaming KV-cache mechanism reduces latency by up to 30% compared to ReKV while maintaining efficient GPU memory usage, proving its effectiveness in managing long video contexts for real-time inference.
solution: StreamAgent introduces an anticipatory agent framework that integrates question semantics and historical observations, using a lightweight MLLM for heuristic-based planning across reactive, proactive, and speculative modes.
