The framework employs tool-augmented action, allowing the agent to proactively select external tools (e.g., "Zoom In," "Object Tracking") to actively acquire critical, task-relevant information from video streams.

A novel streaming Key-Value (KV) cache memory mechanism is proposed for efficient long-context processing, featuring continuous incremental memory updates and selective recall that retrieves only question-relevant KV-caches.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from MBZUAI, Monash University, and other institutions developed StreamAgent, an anticipatory agent for streaming video understanding that integrates proactive temporal and spatial anticipation with a novel streaming Key-Value cache. This framework achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on streaming benchmarks, demonstrating up to 10.7% improvement in "Forward Active Responding" over prior models while reducing latency by up to 30% compared to existing efficient video processing methods.

title: StreamAgent: Towards Anticipatory Agents for Streaming Video Understanding

abstract:
Despite advancements in multimodal large language models (MLLMs), current
approaches struggle in medium-to-long video understanding due to frame and
context length limitations. As a result, these models often depend on frame
sampling, which risks missing key information over time and lacks task-specific
relevance. To address these challenges, we introduce HierarQ, a task-aware
hierarchical Q-Former based framework that sequentially processes frames to
bypass the need for frame sampling, while avoiding LLM's context length
limitations. We introduce a lightweight two-stream language-guided feature
modulator to incorporate task awareness in video understanding, with the entity
stream capturing frame-level object information within a short context and the
scene stream identifying their broader interactions over longer period of time.
Each stream is supported by dedicated memory banks which enables our proposed
Hierachical Querying transformer (HierarQ) to effectively capture short and
long-term context. Extensive evaluations on 10 video benchmarks across video
understanding, question answering, and captioning tasks demonstrate HierarQ's
state-of-the-art performance across most datasets, proving its robustness and
efficiency for comprehensive video analysis.

keyInsights: Auto-regressive processing with intelligent memory management, specifically FIFO for short-term and compression for long-term memory, effectively overcomes context length limitations for arbitrarily long videos.
The two-stream language-guided feature modulator enables dynamic task-aware focus, significantly enhancing feature relevance and overall performance.
Hierarchical integration of entity-level and scene-level contexts through specialized Q-Formers provides a nuanced understanding, outperforming non-hierarchical approaches.
originalProblem: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with long videos due to the quadratic complexity and context length limitations of transformer architectures.
Prevalent frame sampling or compression methods for long videos risk losing crucial temporal information, especially when key details or complex interactions span extended periods.
Current MLLMs often process all video frames uniformly, lacking the ability to prioritize information based on the specific task at hand.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance across 10 video understanding benchmarks, including a 6.8% increase in average top-1 accuracy on the LVU dataset, and improvements of 3.1% on Breakfast and 2.8% on COIN.
Surpassed previous state-of-the-art for long-term VQA (MovieChat-1k) by 3.5% in Global accuracy and 2.9% in Breakpoint accuracy.

Maintained stable performance as video length increased, demonstrating effectiveness in handling extended contexts, and achieved constant memory consumption with linear latency growth, allowing processing of long videos on a 24GB A100 GPU.
solution: HierarQ employs an auto-regressive processing strategy, handling video frames sequentially, and uses dedicated short-term (FIFO) and long-term (Memory Bank Compression) memory banks to retain temporal context efficiently.
A two-stream language-guided feature modulator dynamically emphasizes task-relevant entity-level and scene-level information from video frames based on the text prompt.
A Hierarchical Querying Transformer (HierarQ) integrates fine-grained entity insights into broader scene-level understanding, then feeds this consolidated representation to a frozen LLM.
paper_summary.summary: HierarQ, a task-aware hierarchical Q-Former framework developed at the University of Central Florida's CRCV and Microsoft Research, enhances video understanding for medium-to-long duration content by auto-regressively processing videos with dedicated short-term and long-term memory banks. The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance across 10 diverse video understanding, VQA, and captioning benchmarks, including a 6.8% improvement on the LVU dataset and stable performance with increasing video length.

title: HierarQ: Task-Aware Hierarchical Q-Former for Enhanced Video  Understanding

abstract:
Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly progressed in
offline video understanding. However, applying these models to real-world
scenarios, such as autonomous driving and human-computer interaction, presents
unique challenges due to the need for real-time processing of continuous online
video streams. To this end, this paper presents systematic efforts from three
perspectives: evaluation benchmark, model architecture, and training strategy.
First, we introduce OVBench, a comprehensive question-answering benchmark
designed to evaluate models' ability to perceive, memorize, and reason within
online video contexts. It features 6 core task types across three temporal
contexts-past, current, and future-forming 16 subtasks from diverse datasets.
Second, we propose a new Pyramid Memory Bank (PMB) that effectively retains key
spatiotemporal information in video streams. Third, we proposed an
offline-to-online learning paradigm, designing an interleaved dialogue format
for online video data and constructing an instruction-tuning dataset tailored
for online video training. This framework led to the development of
VideoChat-Online, a robust and efficient model for online video understanding.
Despite the lower computational cost and higher efficiency, VideoChat-Online
outperforms existing state-of-the-art offline and online models across popular
offline video benchmarks and OVBench, demonstrating the effectiveness of our
model architecture and training strategy. % Our approach surpasses existing
state-of-the-art offline models Qwen2-VL 7B and online models Flash-VStream, by
4.19% and 23.7% on OVBench, respectively.

keyInsights: Hierarchical memory, exemplified by the Pyramid Memory Bank (PMB), effectively balances the retention of fine-grained spatial details and long-range temporal patterns, enabling efficient processing of continuous video streams.

A progressive 'offline-to-online' training paradigm, combined with an interleaved dialogue format, significantly enhances a model's ability to perform temporal reasoning and engage in multi-turn dialogues within dynamic online video contexts.

Comprehensive benchmarks like OVBench, designed with distinct temporal contexts (past, current, future) and fine-grained spatio-temporal question types, are necessary to drive the development of truly online-capable video understanding models.
originalProblem: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are primarily designed for offline video understanding, lacking the architectural and training considerations for real-time, continuous video streams.

A lack of comprehensive benchmarks tailored specifically for online video understanding, including dynamic temporal perspectives and time-dependent contexts, hinders systematic evaluation and development.
Prior online video MLLMs struggle with balancing computational efficiency, latency, and the retention of fine-grained spatial and long-range temporal details from infinitely long visual inputs.
results: VideoChat-Online achieved 54.9% accuracy on OVBench, representing a 23.7% improvement over Flash-Vstream (31.2%) and 45.3% over VideoLLM-Online (9.6%) among streaming-capable models.
The model demonstrated superior efficiency, using 8.71 GB of VRAM for 1000 frames compared to MovieChat's 16.90 GB and Flash-VStream's 16.03 GB, while achieving higher accuracy.
VideoChat-Online achieved competitive or superior performance on established offline benchmarks (e.g., MVBench 65.2%, LongVideoBench 54.1%), indicating robust generalization beyond online tasks.
solution: A comprehensive benchmark, OVBench, was developed with 6 core task types and 16 subtasks across 'Current', 'Past', and 'Future' temporal contexts to rigorously evaluate online video understanding.

The Pyramid Memory Bank (PMB) architecture was introduced, featuring a hierarchical structure with progressively increasing sampling rates and decreasing resolutions to efficiently retain spatio-temporal information.

An 'offline-to-online' learning paradigm was implemented, utilizing an interleaved dialogue format for online data and a progressive training approach that first establishes robust offline understanding before integrating online data.
paper_summary.summary: An MLLM designed for online video understanding, VideoChat-Online, is introduced alongside OVBench, a benchmark for evaluating models on continuous video streams. VideoChat-Online achieves 54.9% accuracy on OVBench, outperforming prior streaming-capable models by 23.7% while maintaining high efficiency.

title: Online Video Understanding: OVBench and VideoChat-Online

abstract:
Multimodal social interaction understanding (MMSI) is critical in human-robot
interaction systems. In real-world scenarios, AI agents are required to provide
real-time feedback. However, existing models often depend on both past and
future contexts, which hinders them from applying to real-world problems. To
bridge this gap, we propose an online MMSI setting, where the model must
resolve MMSI tasks using only historical information, such as recorded
dialogues and video streams. To address the challenges of missing the useful
future context, we develop a novel framework, named Online-MMSI-VLM, that
leverages two complementary strategies: multi-party conversation forecasting
and social-aware visual prompting with multi-modal large language models.
First, to enrich linguistic context, the multi-party conversation forecasting
simulates potential future utterances in a coarse-to-fine manner, anticipating
upcoming speaker turns and then generating fine-grained conversational details.
Second, to effectively incorporate visual social cues like gaze and gesture,
social-aware visual prompting highlights the social dynamics in video with
bounding boxes and body keypoints for each person and frame. Extensive
experiments on three tasks and two datasets demonstrate that our method
achieves state-of-the-art performance and significantly outperforms baseline
models, indicating its effectiveness on Online-MMSI. The code and pre-trained
models will be publicly released at: this https URL

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Dallas

title: Towards Online Multi-Modal Social Interaction Understanding

abstract: Real-time understanding of continuous video streams is essential for interactive assistants and multimodal agents operating in dynamic environments. However, most existing video reasoning approaches follow a batch paradigm that defers reasoning until the full video context is observed, resulting in high latency and growing computational cost that are incompatible with streaming scenarios. In this paper, we introduce ThinkStream, a framework for streaming video reasoning based on a Watch--Think--Speak paradigm that enables models to incrementally update their understanding as new video observations arrive. At each step, the model performs a short reasoning update and decides whether sufficient evidence has accumulated to produce a response. To support long-horizon streaming, we propose Reasoning-Compressed Streaming Memory (RCSM), which treats intermediate reasoning traces as compact semantic memory that replaces outdated visual tokens while preserving essential context. We further train the model using a Streaming Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards scheme that aligns incremental reasoning and response timing with the requirements of streaming interaction. Experiments on multiple streaming video benchmarks show that ThinkStream significantly outperforms existing online video models while maintaining low latency and memory usage. Code, models and data will be released at this https URL

keyInsights: Incremental reasoning, where models generate short, localized thoughts, effectively enables real-time understanding and decision-making in dynamic video streams.
Intermediate reasoning traces can serve as a highly compressed and semantically rich long-term memory, significantly mitigating the unbounded growth of visual tokens in long-horizon video processing.

Training with verifiable, rule-based rewards in a streaming reinforcement learning setup successfully guides models to produce accurate, structurally compliant, and temporally precise responses for real-time interaction.
originalProblem: Existing video reasoning models operate on a batch paradigm, leading to high latency and computational costs unsuitable for real-time streaming scenarios.
Current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) face challenges with unbounded KV cache growth and escalating latency when processing continuous, long-horizon video streams.

Models often lack integrated mechanisms for timely interaction and proactive output timing, typically relying on dedicated classification heads rather than generating responses autonomously based on evolving understanding.
results: ThinkStream-3B achieved an average score of 59.66 on OVO-Bench, outperforming its base model Qwen2.5-VL-3B (51.00) and other open-source online models like Streamo-3B (51.64).

The framework successfully bounded end-to-end inference latency below the 0.5s real-time threshold (for 2 FPS inputs), demonstrating practical deployability compared to baseline models with poorly scaling latency.

A custom CUDA Graph-based streaming inference engine achieved over 5x speedup in token decoding (e.g., 154.07 tokens/s vs. 30.06 tokens/s for batch size 1), while maintaining flexible KV cache control.
solution: ThinkStream implements a Watch–Think–Speak paradigm, allowing the model to continuously observe video chunks, perform short incremental reasoning updates, and autonomously decide when to generate a response.
Reasoning-Compressed Streaming Memory (RCSM) manages context by using intermediate reasoning traces as compact semantic memory and dynamically evicting older visual tokens within a sliding window.

Streaming Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) trains the model to generate structured reasoning updates and align response timing with streaming interaction requirements using rule-based rewards for accuracy, format, and temporal alignment.
paper_summary.summary: ThinkStream, a unified framework for real-time video understanding, introduces an incremental Watch–Think–Speak paradigm to process continuous streams efficiently. The framework achieved an average score of 59.66 on OVO-Bench with its 3B model, outperforming existing online models, and successfully reduced end-to-end inference latency below 0.5s for 2 FPS inputs, enabling practical real-time operation.

title: Thinking in Streaming Video

abstract:
Recent developments in Video Large Language Models (Video LLMs) have enabled
models to process long video sequences and demonstrate remarkable performance.
Nonetheless, studies predominantly focus on offline video question answering,
neglecting memory usage and response speed that are essential in various
real-world applications, such as Deepseek services, autonomous driving, and
robotics. To mitigate these challenges, we propose $\textbf{LiveVLM}$, a
training-free framework specifically designed for streaming, online video
understanding and real-time interaction. Unlike existing works that process
videos only after one question is posed, LiveVLM constructs an innovative
streaming-oriented KV cache to process video streams in real-time, retain
long-term video details and eliminate redundant KVs, ensuring prompt responses
to user queries. For continuous video streams, LiveVLM generates and compresses
video key-value tensors (video KVs) to reserve visual information while
improving memory efficiency. Furthermore, when a new question is proposed,
LiveVLM incorporates an online question-answering process that efficiently
fetches both short-term and long-term visual information, while minimizing
interference from redundant context. Extensive experiments demonstrate that
LiveVLM enables the foundation LLaVA-OneVision model to process 44$\times$
number of frames on the same device, and achieves up to 5$\times$ speedup in
response speed compared with SoTA online methods at an input of 256 frames,
while maintaining the same or better model performance.

keyInsights: Efficient management of video information for real-time processing can be achieved by dynamically compressing and storing Key-Value pairs in a streaming-oriented cache.
A retrieval-based mechanism using query attention allows for rapid and relevant context selection from long video streams without processing the entire history.
Applying relative positional embeddings selectively to retrieved tokens addresses the challenge of context window limitations for extremely long sequences in an online setting.
originalProblem: Most existing Video LLMs operate in an offline setting, requiring complete video processing before generating responses, which limits their use in real-time applications.
Traditional Video LLMs exhibit quadratic computational complexity (O(n²)) and high memory overhead for long video sequences, making them impractical for continuous streams.
Significant response latency and challenges in retaining relevant information while discarding redundant data hinder effective real-time interaction with video streams.
results: LiveVLM achieves up to a 5x speedup in response latency and a 2.6x reduction in memory consumption compared to existing methods.
The framework maintains performance while processing significantly longer videos and shows consistent scalability improvements as video length increases.
LiveVLM demonstrates state-of-the-art results on StreamingBench, particularly excelling in multimodal alignment and long video understanding scenarios.
solution: LiveVLM introduces a training-free framework that integrates a streaming-oriented Key-Value (KV) cache system and an online question-answering process.
The streaming-oriented KV cache uses video-specific KV compression (discarding low-attention pairs, merging frame-wise) and a FIFO-managed long-term memory for efficient information retention.
An online question-answering process employs query-based retrieval to select relevant historical chunks from the KV cache and applies relative positional embedding for long sequence handling.
paper_summary.summary: LiveVLM introduces a training-free framework that enables Video Large Language Models to process continuous video streams in real-time by utilizing a streaming-oriented Key-Value (KV) cache and an efficient retrieval mechanism. This approach reduces memory consumption by 2.6x and offers up to a 5x speedup in response latency compared to prior methods.

title: LiveVLM: Efficient Online Video Understanding via Streaming-Oriented KV  Cache and Retrieval

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown strong performance on offline video understanding, but most are limited to offline inference or have weak online reasoning, making multi-turn interaction over continuously arriving video streams difficult. Existing streaming methods typically use an interleaved perception-generation paradigm, which prevents concurrent perception and generation and leads to early memory decay as streams grow, hurting long-range dependency modeling. We propose Think While Watching, a memory-anchored streaming video reasoning framework that preserves continuous segment-level memory during multi-turn interaction. We build a three-stage, multi-round chain-of-thought dataset and adopt a stage-matched training strategy, while enforcing strict causality through a segment-level streaming causal mask and streaming positional encoding. During inference, we introduce an efficient pipeline that overlaps watching and thinking and adaptively selects the best attention backend. Under both single-round and multi-round streaming input protocols, our method achieves strong results. Built on Qwen3-VL, it improves single-round accuracy by 2.6% on StreamingBench and by 3.79% on OVO-Bench. In the multi-round setting, it maintains performance while reducing output tokens by 56%. Code is available at: this https URL

keyInsights: Explicit segment-level memory notes effectively condense and maintain long-range context, demonstrating improved accuracy and reduced token usage in multi-turn video reasoning.

Decoupling video ingestion from text generation through architectural innovations mitigates the "Serialization Bottleneck," substantially reducing Time to First Token and improving real-time responsiveness.

A three-stage streaming-aligned training strategy, incorporating a novel multi-round Chain-of-Thought dataset, is crucial for teaching MLLMs causal online reasoning and also improves performance on offline video understanding benchmarks.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) experience a substantial performance drop when adapting from "offline" (full video available) to "online" (streaming, real-time) multi-turn video understanding.

Current streaming MLLMs using interleaved perception-generation face "Memory Erosion," losing long-range context in multi-turn dialogues, and a "Serialization Bottleneck," where text generation pauses video processing, increasing latency.
Maintaining strict causality, where responses are based only on previously observed video content and dialogue, is challenging in streaming environments.
results: TWW consistently improved online single-round accuracy, with Qwen3-VL-4B showing a 1.52% gain on StreamingBench and a 4.32% gain on OVO-Bench compared to the Thinking baseline.

In multi-turn online settings, TWW achieved 57.40% accuracy on StreamingBench with a 56.10% reduction in average output tokens and lowered Time to First Token by 92.6% compared to the batch Thinking baseline.

Training for long-range capabilities (Stage 3) shifted attention distribution from recent video frames to distant memory tokens, demonstrating enhanced long-term memory recall for multi-turn interactions.
solution: A "Think While Watching" (TWW) framework that generates compact, segment-level textual memory notes (mt) for continuously arriving video segments, which are stored in a persistent memory bank.

A modified MLLM architecture incorporating a segment-level streaming attention mask and decoupled streaming positional encoding (MRoPE) to enforce strict causality and support concurrent video processing and text generation.

A three-stage fine-tuning strategy, alongside a newly synthesized multi-round Chain-of-Thought (CoT) dataset, to train the model on causal processing, multi-turn consistency, and long-range temporal reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: The "Think While Watching" (TWW) framework enables multimodal large language models to perform multi-turn video reasoning on continuous streams by using segment-level memory notes and a decoupled perception-generation architecture. This approach yielded consistent accuracy improvements in online settings, reduced output tokens by up to 56.10% in multi-turn interactions, and lowered Time to First Token by 92.6% compared to baselines.

title: Think While Watching: Online Streaming Segment-Level Memory for Multi-Turn Video Reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved strong performance across many tasks, yet most systems remain limited to offline inference, requiring complete inputs before generating outputs. Recent streaming methods reduce latency by interleaving perception and generation, but still enforce a sequential perception-generation cycle, limiting real-time interaction. In this work, we target a fundamental bottleneck that arises when extending MLLMs to real-time video understanding: the global positional continuity constraint imposed by standard positional encoding schemes. While natural in offline inference, this constraint tightly couples perception and generation, preventing effective input-output parallelism. To address this limitation, we propose a parallel streaming framework that relaxes positional continuity through three designs: Overlapped, Group-Decoupled, and Gap-Isolated. These designs enable simultaneous perception and generation, allowing the model to process incoming inputs while producing responses in real time. Extensive experiments reveal that Group-Decoupled achieves the best efficiency-performance balance, maintaining high fluency and accuracy while significantly reducing latency. We further show that the proposed framework yields up to 2x acceleration under balanced perception-generation workloads, establishing a principled pathway toward speak-while-watching real-time systems. We make all our code publicly available: this https URL.

keyInsights: The global positional continuity constraint in MLLMs is a primary barrier to true real-time input-output parallelism, and relaxing it is crucial for enabling simultaneous perception and generation.
Conventional interleaved streaming methods significantly degrade linguistic fluency and coherence in generated text due to interruptions from incoming visual frames.

Decoupling input and output positional encoding spaces, particularly with Group-Decoupled Position Encoding (GDPE), offers the best balance of task accuracy, linguistic fluency, and robustness in streaming scenarios.
originalProblem: Current Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) operate under an "offline inference" paradigm, processing entire videos before responding, causing high latency.

Existing "streaming MLLMs" use an "interleaved" approach, alternating between input perception and output generation, which still results in latency accumulation and unresponsiveness in dynamic environments.
A fundamental bottleneck preventing true real-time input-output parallelism is the global positional continuity constraint in standard MLLM positional encoding schemes.
results: Group-Decoupled Position Encoding (GDPE) provided the most balanced performance across streaming video description and QA tasks, showing significantly better linguistic fluency than interleaved methods.

The proposed strategies (OSPE, GDPE, GIPE) demonstrated strong robustness to irregular streaming conditions, maintaining stable and readable outputs, unlike the conventional interleaved method which produced fragmented phrases.
Theoretical analysis indicates that parallel streaming can achieve a theoretical speedup of up to 2x, especially when perception and generation workloads are balanced.
solution: The research introduces a parallel streaming framework that relaxes the global positional continuity constraint in MLLMs, enabling simultaneous encoding of visual inputs and decoding of textual outputs.
Three positional encoding strategies were developed: Overlapped Streaming Position Encoding (OSPE), Group-Decoupled Position Encoding (GDPE), and Gap-Isolated Position Encoding (GIPE).
The proposed methods integrate into existing MLLM architectures (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL) as "plug-and-play" components, focusing on redesigning positional encoding rather than core model architecture.
paper_summary.summary: A new framework enables Multimodal Large Language Models to achieve true real-time video understanding by addressing the global positional continuity constraint inherent in current designs. The Group-Decoupled Position Encoding (GDPE) strategy allows simultaneous visual input processing and textual output generation, leading to improved linguistic fluency and robustness under irregular streaming conditions, with a theoretical speedup of up to 2x.

title: Speak While Watching: Unleashing TRUE Real-Time Video Understanding Capability of Multimodal Large Language Models

abstract: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models have greatly improved visual understanding and reasoning, yet their quadratic attention and offline training protocols make them ill-suited for streaming settings where frames arrive sequentially and future observations are inaccessible. We diagnose a core limitation of current Video-LLMs, namely Time-Agnosticism, in which videos are treated as an unordered bag of evidence rather than a causally ordered sequence, yielding two failures in streams: temporal order ambiguity, in which the model cannot follow or reason over the correct chronological order, and past-current focus blindness where it fails to distinguish present observations from accumulated history. We present WeaveTime, a simple, efficient, and model agnostic framework that first teaches order and then uses order. We introduce a lightweight Temporal Reconstruction objective-our Streaming Order Perception enhancement-that instills order aware representations with minimal finetuning and no specialized streaming data. At inference, a Past-Current Dynamic Focus Cache performs uncertainty triggered, coarse-to-fine retrieval, expanding history only when needed. Plugged into exsiting Video-LLM without architectural changes, WeaveTime delivers consistent gains on representative streaming benchmarks, improving accuracy while reducing latency. These results establish WeaveTime as a practical path toward time aware stream Video-LLMs under strict online, time causal constraints. Code and weights will be made publicly available. Project Page: this https URL

keyInsights: Current Video-LLMs exhibit "Time-Agnosticism," often performing similarly on shuffled and ordered video frames, highlighting their lack of genuine temporal understanding.
Explicitly training Video-LLMs to reconstruct the correct chronological order of shuffled, timestamped video segments effectively instills temporal awareness.
An uncertainty-gated, coarse-to-fine memory retrieval strategy enables efficient and targeted access to historical context, dynamically balancing current observations with past information.
originalProblem: Existing Video-LLMs are "Time-Agnostic," treating video frames as an unordered collection, hindering their performance in real-time streaming environments.
This results in two key issues: Temporal Order Ambiguity (inability to interpret event chronology) and Past–Current Focus Blindness (ineffective attention to current vs. historical context).
Prior approaches for streaming video understanding often require extensive specialized training data, costly retraining, or suffer from efficiency issues.
results: WeaveTime consistently improves accuracy on streaming benchmarks (OVO-Bench Real-Time, Streaming-Bench Real-Time) by up to 7.10% over competitive baselines, showing particular gains in temporal reasoning tasks like Action Perception (+7.56%).

The framework operates with high data and compute efficiency, utilizing only 30K offline video samples and 8 GPUs for training, in contrast to other methods requiring significantly more resources and specialized streaming data.

The Past–Current Dynamic Focus Cache (PCDF-Cache) with its Coarse-to-Fine retrieval strategy enhances both recall and accuracy on long-form video QA while preventing out-of-memory errors and reducing latency through adaptive memory access.
solution: A two-stage "teach order, then use order" framework is introduced, comprising a training enhancement and an inference-time memory mechanism.
Streaming Order Perception Enhancement (SOPE) teaches temporal order via a Temporal Reconstruction auxiliary task, where the LLM re-orders shuffled, timestamped frames.
Past–Current Dynamic Focus Cache (PCDF-Cache) employs an uncertainty-gated recall policy and Coarse-to-Fine (C2F) retrieval to efficiently manage dynamic memory during inference.
paper_summary.summary: The WeaveTime framework enhances Video-LLMs by instilling genuine temporal order perception and dynamic memory management for streaming video understanding. This approach significantly improves accuracy and efficiency on streaming benchmarks, enabling models to reason about chronological event sequences in real-time.

title: WeaveTime: Stream from Earlier Frames into Emergent Memory in VideoLLMs

abstract: Recent advances in video multimodal large language models (Video MLLMs) have significantly enhanced video understanding and multi-modal interaction capabilities. While most existing systems operate in a turn-based manner where the model can only reply after user turns, proactively deciding when to reply during video playback presents a promising yet challenging direction for real-time applications. In this work, we propose a novel text-to-text approach to proactive interaction, where the model autonomously determines whether to respond or remain silent at each turn based on dialogue history and visual context up to current frame of an streaming video. To overcome difficulties in previous methods such as manually tuning response decision thresholds and annotating precise reply times, we introduce a multi-turn RL based training method that encourages timely and accurate responses without requiring precise response time annotations. We train our model MMDuet2 on a dataset of 52k videos with two types of dialogues via SFT and RL. Experimental results demonstrate that MMDuet2 outperforms existing proactive Video MLLM baselines in response timing and quality, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the ProactiveVideoQA benchmark.

keyInsights: Representing proactive interaction as a text-to-text task allows the model to autonomously decide on response timing and content, removing the need for manual threshold tuning.

Reinforcement learning with a custom, multi-faceted reward system is effective for teaching timely and relevant proactive responses, overcoming the challenge of precise temporal annotation in training data.
Maintaining a lower frame sampling rate during supervised fine-tuning prevents model collapse, while a higher rate during inference significantly enhances proactive response timeliness and quality.
originalProblem: Existing proactive Video MLLMs rely on manually tuned response thresholds, which are sensitive and lead to inconsistent or redundant replies.
Training proactive interaction models requires precise reply timestamp annotations, which are difficult, costly, and prone to inaccuracies.
Prior RL-enhanced Video MLLMs had not explored real-time, multi-turn dialogue, limiting their application in truly interactive scenarios.
results: MMDUET2 (RL-trained) achieved state-of-the-art performance on the ProactiveVideoQA benchmark, reaching a PAUC of 53.3 and a 4.2% duplicate reply proportion on the [WEB] dataset, outperforming MMDuet's 38.9 PAUC and 81.3% duplication.

The model maintained performance nearly identical to its base Qwen2.5-VL 3B model on standard offline video understanding benchmarks (Video-MME, MVBench, LongVideoBench), demonstrating no catastrophic forgetting.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of each reward component (replication, in-span, prefix rewards) in preventing issues such as redundant responses, out-of-context replies, and verbose output.
solution: MMDUET2 formulates proactive interaction as a text-to-text generation task, where the model explicitly outputs a textual response or "NO REPLY," eliminating manual thresholds.

A multi-turn reinforcement learning (RL) framework is introduced, employing a specialized reward system (PAUC, replication, in-span, prefix rewards) to encourage timely and accurate responses without precise timestamp annotations.
A large-scale, high-quality proactive dialogue dataset was constructed from web and ego-centric videos to facilitate robust training and diverse interaction scenarios.
paper_summary.summary: MMDUET2, a Video MLLM developed by Peking University and Meituan, improves proactive interaction by using multi-turn reinforcement learning and a text-to-text chat template to autonomously determine when and what to respond during streaming video. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on proactive interaction benchmarks, with a PAUC of 53.3 on the WEB dataset and significantly reduced reply duplication, while maintaining general video understanding capabilities.

title: MMDuet2: Enhancing Proactive Interaction of Video MLLMs with Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Streaming video understanding often involves time-sensitive scenarios where models need to answer exactly when the supporting visual evidence appears: answering before the evidence reflects speculation, answering after it has passed reduces real-time utility. To capture this behavior, we introduce a readiness-aware formulation of streaming video understanding with the Answer Readiness Score (ARS), a timing-aware objective with asymmetric early and late penalties. When combined with correctness, ARS defines an effective accuracy that measures not just whether a model is right, but whether it answers at the appropriate moment. Building on this formulation, we introduce StreamReady, a framework to unify temporal reasoning with on-time answering through a lightweight readiness mechanism that decides if sufficient evidence has been observed before responding. To evaluate this capability, we further introduce ProReady-QA, a benchmark with annotated answer evidence windows and proactive multi-turn questions across local and global contexts. StreamReady achieves superior performance on ProReady-QA, and consistently outperforms prior methods across eight additional streaming and offline long-video benchmarks, demonstrating robust and broadly generalizable video understanding capability.

keyInsights: Explicitly learning a 'readiness' signal alongside content understanding enables models to provide timely, evidence-grounded answers in streaming scenarios, reducing unsupported speculation and enhancing real-time utility.
A multi-level hierarchical visual memory effectively captures long-range temporal context in streaming videos, facilitating robust reasoning without prohibitive computational costs.

Asymmetric penalties within the ARS metric effectively guide model training to prioritize avoiding premature (speculative) answers while tolerating slight delays, aligning with real-world application demands.
originalProblem: Existing long-video understanding methods operate offline, making them unsuitable for real-time streaming where models must process sequential frames without foresight.
Current streaming video understanding largely focuses on past-dependent reasoning, overlooking the crucial timing of responses for future-dependent (proactive) questions.
A lack of explicit annotations for answer evidence durations in existing proactive benchmarks hinders the systematic evaluation of temporal appropriateness in model responses.
results: StreamReady achieved the highest accuracy and Answer Readiness Score (ARS) on the ProReady-QA benchmark, surpassing the best competing model by approximately 3% in accuracy and 9% in ARS on average across proactive tasks.

The framework consistently outperformed prior models on other streaming video understanding benchmarks, including StreamingBench and VStream-QA, with gains of up to approximately 5% on proactive tasks.
StreamReady demonstrated stable latency and memory usage even with increasing video lengths, attributing this efficiency to its fixed-size compact memory and lightweight readiness mechanism.
solution: Formalized "readiness-aware streaming video understanding" and introduced the Answer Readiness Score (ARS), a novel timing-aware evaluation metric with asymmetric penalties for early and late responses.

Developed StreamReady, a framework featuring a hierarchical visual memory for long-range context and a lightweight, learnable readiness mechanism that signals when sufficient visual evidence is present to generate an answer.

Created ProReady-QA, a new benchmark comprising long streaming videos, proactive multi-turn questions, and precise annotations of ground-truth answer evidence windows for systematic temporal assessment.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the University of Central Florida and Microsoft Research developed StreamReady, a framework for "readiness-aware streaming video understanding" that determines when to answer questions based on visual evidence. StreamReady, coupled with a new metric (Answer Readiness Score, ARS) and benchmark (ProReady-QA), achieved superior performance on proactive tasks, outperforming competing models by approximately 3% in accuracy and 9% in ARS on ProReady-QA.

title: StreamReady: Learning What to Answer and When in Long Streaming Videos

abstract:
The rapid advancements in large language models (LLMs) have spurred growing
interest in LLM-based video anomaly detection (VAD). However, existing
approaches predominantly focus on video-level anomaly question answering or
offline detection, ignoring the real-time nature essential for practical VAD
applications. To bridge this gap and facilitate the practical deployment of
LLM-based VAD, we introduce AssistPDA, the first online video anomaly
surveillance assistant that unifies video anomaly prediction, detection, and
analysis (VAPDA) within a single framework. AssistPDA enables real-time
inference on streaming videos while supporting interactive user engagement.
Notably, we introduce a novel event-level anomaly prediction task, enabling
proactive anomaly forecasting before anomalies fully unfold. To enhance the
ability to model intricate spatiotemporal relationships in anomaly events, we
propose a Spatio-Temporal Relation Distillation (STRD) module. STRD transfers
the long-term spatiotemporal modeling capabilities of vision-language models
(VLMs) from offline settings to real-time scenarios. Thus it equips AssistPDA
with a robust understanding of complex temporal dependencies and long-sequence
memory. Additionally, we construct VAPDA-127K, the first large-scale benchmark
designed for VLM-based online VAPDA. Extensive experiments demonstrate that
AssistPDA outperforms existing offline VLM-based approaches, setting a new
state-of-the-art for real-time VAPDA. Our dataset and code will be open-sourced
to facilitate further research in the community.
title: AssistPDA: An Online Video Surveillance Assistant for Video Anomaly  Prediction, Detection, and Analysis

abstract: The rapid development of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has expanded their capabilities from image comprehension to video understanding. However, most of these MLLMs focus primarily on offline video comprehension, necessitating extensive processing of all video frames before any queries can be made. This presents a significant gap compared to the human ability to watch, listen, think, and respond to streaming inputs in real time, highlighting the limitations of current MLLMs. In this paper, we introduce StreamingBench, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate the streaming video understanding capabilities of MLLMs. StreamingBench assesses three core aspects of streaming video understanding: (1) real-time visual understanding, (2) omni-source understanding, and (3) contextual understanding. The benchmark consists of 18 tasks, featuring 900 videos and 4,500 human-curated QA pairs. Each video features five questions presented at different time points to simulate a continuous streaming scenario. We conduct experiments on StreamingBench with 13 open-source and proprietary MLLMs and find that even the most advanced proprietary MLLMs like Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT-4o perform significantly below human-level streaming video understanding capabilities. We hope our work can facilitate further advancements for MLLMs, empowering them to approach human-level video comprehension and interaction in more realistic scenarios.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Current MLLMs exhibit substantial performance gaps compared to human understanding, particularly in omni-source and contextual understanding tasks.
Model performance is sensitive to video length, with declines noted for longer sequences, and struggles when processing redundant or misleading information.
Temporal reasoning remains a challenge, as models perform worse when critical information appears after the query timestamp or when explicit reference resolution is lacking.
originalProblem: Current Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are primarily evaluated on static images and offline videos, lacking comprehensive benchmarks for real-time streaming video understanding.

Existing video understanding benchmarks assume full video availability, which fundamentally differs from streaming scenarios where models must process partial information sequentially under temporal constraints.
Real-world applications such as live video conferencing and autonomous driving require MLLMs to comprehend dynamic, incoming video streams in real-time.
results: Even the top-performing MLLM, Gemini 1.5 Pro, achieves substantially lower accuracy than human benchmarks across all task categories in StreamingBench.

Models perform relatively better on real-time visual understanding tasks but struggle significantly with omni-source (e.g., multimodal alignment) and contextual understanding (e.g., causal reasoning) challenges.
The proactive output task reveals particular weaknesses, with most models failing to accurately identify relevant moments and provide timely responses according to instructions.
solution: StreamingBench is introduced as a comprehensive benchmark with 900 videos and 4,500 question-answer pairs designed to evaluate MLLMs on streaming video understanding.
The benchmark organizes evaluation tasks into three main categories: Real-Time Visual Understanding, Omni-Source Understanding, and Contextual Understanding.
Data construction combines automated generation using GPT-4o for visual tasks with human curation for more complex omni-source and contextual tasks, ensuring quality and relevance.
paper_summary.summary: StreamingBench is presented as a new benchmark for evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) on streaming video understanding, addressing the limitations of existing benchmarks that focus on offline video analysis. The evaluation of 13 MLLMs reveals significant performance gaps between current models and human-level understanding, particularly in tasks requiring complex contextual and omni-source integration.

title: StreamingBench: Assessing the Gap for MLLMs to Achieve Streaming Video Understanding

abstract: Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) exhibit strong Chain-of-Thought (CoT) capabilities, yet most existing paradigms assume full-video availability before inference, a batch-style process misaligned with real-world video streams where information arrives sequentially. Motivated by the streaming nature of video data, we investigate two streaming reasoning paradigms for LVLMs. The first, an interleaved paradigm, alternates between receiving frames and producing partial reasoning but remains constrained by strictly ordered cache updates. To better match streaming inputs, we propose \textbf{Think-as-You-See (TaYS)}, a unified framework enabling true concurrent reasoning. TaYS integrates parallelized CoT generation, stream-constrained training, and stream-parallel inference. It further employs temporally aligned reasoning units, streaming attention masks and positional encodings, and a dual KV-cache that decouples visual encoding from textual reasoning. We evaluate all paradigms on the Qwen2.5-VL family across representative video CoT tasks, including event dynamics analysis, causal reasoning, and thematic understanding. Experiments show that TaYS consistently outperforms both batch and interleaved baselines, improving reasoning performance while substantially reducing time-to-first-token (TTFT) and overall reasoning delay. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of data-aligned streaming reasoning in enabling efficient and responsive video understanding for LVLMs. We release our code at this https URL

keyInsights: Decoupling visual ingestion and linguistic reasoning via modality-specific caches and parallel processing is critical for achieving true concurrency and low latency in streaming LVLMs.
Strict temporal causality, enforced through tailored attention masks and positional encoding, ensures that reasoning steps are precisely aligned with unfolding visual evidence.
Supervised fine-tuning with carefully constructed streaming-style video CoT trajectories can effectively adapt batch-oriented LVLMs to perform incremental and temporally grounded reasoning.
originalProblem: Most LVLMs process video in a batch inference paradigm, introducing significant latency unsuitable for real-time applications.
Long videos processed in batch mode can lead to 'temporal drift,' where models lose track of early visual cues, causing hallucinations.
Naive streaming approaches often create new computational bottlenecks or sacrifice fine-grained temporal alignment for efficiency.
results: TaYS achieved the highest subjective evaluation ranking, with a 43.7% normalized win rate (by GPT-5) for logical consistency and contextual appropriateness, outperforming batch (31.4%) and interleaved (21.7%) baselines.
It demonstrated near-zero Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) at the decoder level (approx. 10⁻⁶ seconds) and maintained a stable end-to-end delay of approximately 12 seconds across various frame rates.

TaYS showed superior temporal grounding with a mean deviation of 0.69 seconds between reasoning steps and keyframes (compared to 1.52 seconds for interleaved), with 86.0% of reasoning outputs falling within one second of event boundaries.
solution: The Think-as-You-See (TaYS) framework was introduced, employing supervised fine-tuning to adapt LVLMs for streaming video Chain-of-Thought reasoning.
A parallel streaming paradigm was developed, using a Streaming Attention Mask and Streaming Positional Encoding to enforce temporal causality and independent indexing.
A Parallel Dual KV-Cache mechanism was implemented, featuring independent, asynchronously updated video and text caches to decouple perception from reasoning, enabling concurrent execution.
paper_summary.summary: Think-as-You-See (TaYS) is a framework developed to enable Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to perform streaming Chain-of-Thought reasoning for video analysis. It achieves near-zero Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) at the decoder level and improved temporal grounding, allowing LVLMs to process visual streams incrementally and concurrently.

title: Think-as-You-See: Streaming Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for Large Vision-Language Models

abstract: Real-time understanding of long video streams remains challenging for multimodal large language models (VLMs) due to redundant frame processing and rapid forgetting of past context. Existing streaming systems rely on fixed-interval decoding or cache pruning, which either produce repetitive outputs or discard crucial temporal information. We introduce Event-VStream, an event-aware framework that represents continuous video as a sequence of discrete, semantically coherent events. Our system detects meaningful state transitions by integrating motion, semantic, and predictive cues, and triggers language generation only at those boundaries. Each event embedding is consolidated into a persistent memory bank, enabling long-horizon reasoning while maintaining low latency. Across OVOBench-Realtime, and long-form Ego4D evaluations, Event-VStream achieves competitive performance. It improves over a VideoLLM-Online-8B baseline by +10.4 points on OVOBench-Realtime, achieves performance close to Flash-VStream-7B despite using only a general-purpose LLaMA-3-8B text backbone, and maintains around 70% GPT-5 win rate on 2-hour Ego4D streams.

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Arlington

keyInsights: Video content exhibits 'block-structured recurrence' in visual embeddings, where semantics remain stable within segments and change abruptly at boundaries, validating an event-centric processing approach.
Designing VLM processing to align with human cognitive segmentation of continuous experience into discrete events leads to more efficient and coherent understanding.
Integrating multiple complementary cues (semantic drift, motion, prediction error) is crucial for robust and accurate real-time detection of meaningful event boundaries.
originalProblem: Existing streaming VLM systems process frames uniformly, leading to high redundancy, repetitive outputs, and wasted computational resources over long videos.

Current methods for managing long video streams often employ fixed-interval KV cache refreshing or sparsification, which risk premature discarding of crucial temporal information and cause models to 'forget' long-term context.
Developing VLMs that can maintain coherent, low-latency understanding for unbounded, multi-hour video streams poses challenges for maintaining contextual stability and computational efficiency.
results: Demonstrated superior long-term stability on 2-hour Ego4D streams, achieving an average GPT-5 win rate of 70%, and up to 88.3% in later segments, significantly outperforming existing streaming VLM baselines.
Achieved an absolute gain of +10.4 points on the OVOBench-Realtime benchmark, scoring 28.15, representing a substantial accuracy improvement over its VideoLLM-Online-8B baseline.
Maintained real-time, stable sub-0.1 s/token latency across entire 2-hour streams, effectively avoiding the latency drift and out-of-memory failures observed in frame-centric processing methods.
solution: The Event-VStream framework converts continuous video into a dynamic sequence of discrete, semantically coherent events using an Event Boundary Detector, Event Memory, and Event-Driven Decoder.
An Event Boundary Detector combines semantic drift, motion cues, and prediction error to causally identify meaningful state transitions and aggregate relevant frames into boundary-aware event tokens.

An Event Memory stores consolidated event embeddings, using a 'merge-or-append' rule to maintain a compact, persistent context, while the Event-Driven Decoder generates text only at detected event boundaries with controlled pacing.
paper_summary.summary: Event-VStream introduces an event-driven framework that transforms continuous video streams into discrete, semantically coherent events for real-time understanding by multimodal large language models. The system significantly improves long-term context retention and achieves substantial gains in performance on video understanding benchmarks while maintaining real-time latency.

title: Event-VStream: Event-Driven Real-Time Understanding for Long Video Streams

abstract: Recent researches on video large language models (VideoLLM) predominantly focus on model architectures and training datasets, leaving the interaction format between the user and the model under-explored. In existing works, users often interact with VideoLLMs by using the entire video and a query as input, after which the model generates a response. This interaction format constrains the application of VideoLLMs in scenarios such as live-streaming comprehension where videos do not end and responses are required in a real-time manner, and also results in unsatisfactory performance on time-sensitive tasks that requires localizing video segments. In this paper, we focus on a video-text duet interaction format. This interaction format is characterized by the continuous playback of the video, and both the user and the model can insert their text messages at any position during the video playback. When a text message ends, the video continues to play, akin to the alternative of two performers in a duet. We construct MMDuetIT, a video-text training dataset designed to adapt VideoLLMs to video-text duet interaction format. We also introduce the Multi-Answer Grounded Video Question Answering (MAGQA) task to benchmark the real-time response ability of VideoLLMs. Trained on MMDuetIT, MMDuet demonstrates that adopting the video-text duet interaction format enables the model to achieve significant improvements in various time-sensitive tasks (76% CIDEr on YouCook2 dense video captioning, 90\% mAP on QVHighlights highlight detection and 25% R@0.5 on Charades-STA temporal video grounding) with minimal training efforts, and also enable VideoLLMs to reply in a real-time manner as the video plays.

organization_info.name: Beijing Institute for General Artificial Intelligence

keyInsights: Empowering VideoLLMs to autonomously decide *when* to respond in a continuous video stream significantly enhances their capability for real-time, time-sensitive video comprehension.

Using dedicated classification heads to predict informativeness and relevance for each frame directly addresses the challenge of precise temporal grounding, bypassing the difficulties of LLMs generating accurate numerical time indicators.
Efficient adaptation of a powerful VideoLLM to a complex new interaction paradigm is possible with a relatively small, carefully curated dataset, as demonstrated by MMDuetIT's effectiveness.
originalProblem: Existing VideoLLMs typically process entire videos before responding, which prevents timely interactions necessary for real-time applications like live-streaming analysis.

Previous models struggled with time-sensitive tasks requiring precise temporal localization (e.g., temporal video grounding) because large language models (LLMs) are inefficient at generating accurate numeric timestamps.

The traditional "whole video" interaction paradigm limits the ability of VideoLLMs to engage in dynamic, multi-turn conversations and provide contextually relevant responses in continuous video streams.
results: MMDuet achieved substantial gains on highlight detection (e.g., 31.3 mAP on QVHighlights vs. 19.0 for LLaVA-OV-VT) and temporal video grounding (e.g., 42.4 R@0.5 on Charades-STA vs. 36.5 for LLaVA-OV-VT).

Performance on dense video captioning improved (e.g., 8.8 CIDEr on YouCook2 vs. 6.7 for LLaVA-OV-VT), and the model showed superior real-time multi-answer grounded QA capabilities on the MAGQA task, especially for longer videos.

The model outperformed other streaming and proactive MLLMs on the 'Proactive Output' task of StreamingBench, validating its ability to deliver timely responses (e.g., 31.85 accuracy within two seconds of relevant scene).
solution: A novel "video-text duet interaction format" was formalized, allowing a VideoLLM to autonomously decide when to interject with text responses while a video plays continuously, treating the video stream as a conversational participant.

The MMDuet model was developed, extending a standard VideoLLM architecture with specialized 'informative' and 'relevance' heads that enable autonomous, real-time response generation after processing each video frame.

A new training dataset, MMDuetIT, was constructed by reformatting existing video-text datasets and generating data for a novel 'Multi-Answer Grounded Video Question Answering (MAGQA)' task, specifically to train models for this duet interaction.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University, Huawei Noah's Ark Lab, and Beijing Institute for General Artificial Intelligence introduced a "video-text duet interaction format" for VideoLLMs, enabling models to decide when to respond within continuous video streams. Their MMDuet model, trained with the MMDuetIT dataset, substantially improves performance on time-sensitive video comprehension tasks like highlight detection (31.3 mAP on QVHighlights vs. 19.0 for baselines) and temporal video grounding (42.4 R@0.5 on Charades-STA vs. 36.5 for baselines), while also showing superior real-time question answering on the novel MAGQA task.

title: VideoLLM Knows When to Speak: Enhancing Time-Sensitive Video Comprehension with Video-Text Duet Interaction Format

abstract: The rapid advancement of multimodal large language models has demonstrated impressive capabilities, yet nearly all operate in an offline paradigm, hindering real-time interactivity. Addressing this gap, we introduce the Real-tIme Video intERaction Bench (RIVER Bench), designed for evaluating online video comprehension. RIVER Bench introduces a novel framework comprising Retrospective Memory, Live-Perception, and Proactive Anticipation tasks, closely mimicking interactive dialogues rather than responding to entire videos at once. We conducted detailed annotations using videos from diverse sources and varying lengths, and precisely defined the real-time interactive format. Evaluations across various model categories reveal that while offline models perform well in single question-answering tasks, they struggle with real-time processing. Addressing the limitations of existing models in online video interaction, especially their deficiencies in long-term memory and future perception, we proposed a general improvement method that enables models to interact with users more flexibly in real time. We believe this work will significantly advance the development of real-time interactive video understanding models and inspire future research in this emerging field. Datasets and code are publicly available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Effective online MLLMs (oMLLMs) require strong long-term memory retention, precise temporal grounding for current events, and proactive reasoning to anticipate future actions.

Existing "online" MLLMs often underperform in truly interactive, dynamic streaming scenarios, suggesting their optimization for general long-video comprehension or specific offline QA formats rather than continuous interaction.

Adapting offline MLLMs with a sliding window and LSTMM can significantly enhance their performance on interactive tasks, demonstrating a practical pathway to bridge the gap between offline and online capabilities.
originalProblem: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) primarily operate in an "offline" paradigm, processing entire videos before responding, which is unsuitable for real-time interactive applications.
Current benchmarks for Video LLMs inadequately evaluate crucial online interaction capabilities such as long-term memory degradation, precise temporal grounding, and proactive reasoning.
There is a lack of a comprehensive and standardized framework to rigorously assess how Video LLMs perform in continuous, human-like dialogues with streaming visual inputs.
results: Open-source models, when adapted with the proposed online inference pipeline, achieved highly competitive results on RIVER Bench, even outperforming some native multimodal inference approaches on Live-Perception tasks.
Fine-tuning VideoLLM-Online on RIVER Bench's specialized training data led to an 11.28% accuracy gain in proactive response tasks, validating the data's effectiveness for online interaction.

Models demonstrated performance decay in visual memory for Retro-Memory questions as recall duration increased, but the addition of memory modules reduced this decay by 12% and improved retention stability within one hour compared to models without memory.
solution: Introduces RIVER Bench, a novel evaluation framework with three task types: Retro-Memory (past events), Live-Perception (current events), and Pro-Response (future events), to comprehensively assess real-time interactive capabilities.

Develops a meticulous data construction process, curating content from diverse video datasets and employing LLM-assisted and human filtering to ensure high-quality, unbiased, and temporally precise question-answer pairs.

Proposes a general online adaptation method for offline MLLMs, utilizing a sliding window approach, a Long-Short Term Memory (LSTMM) module for historical context, and prompt engineering to provide temporal information.
paper_summary.summary: RIVER Bench introduces a real-time interaction evaluation framework for Video Large Language Models (LLMs), featuring three distinct task types: Retro-Memory, Live-Perception, and Pro-Response, to assess their ability to interact with streaming video. The benchmark reveals current MLLMs' limitations in dynamic scenarios and provides a method to adapt offline models, achieving improved performance on interactive tasks.

title: RIVER: A Real-Time Interaction Benchmark for Video LLMs

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have made rapid progress in perception, understanding, and reasoning, yet existing benchmarks fall short in evaluating these abilities under continuous and dynamic real-world video streams. Such settings require models to maintain coherent understanding and reasoning as visual scenes evolve over time. **We introduce RTV-Bench, a fine-grained benchmark for real-time video analysis with MLLMs**. It is built upon three key principles: multi-timestamp question answering, hierarchical question structures spanning perception and reasoning, and multi-dimensional evaluation of continuous perception, understanding, and reasoning. RTV-Bench comprises 552 diverse videos and 4,608 carefully curated QA pairs covering a wide range of dynamic scenarios. We evaluate a broad range of state-of-the-art MLLMs, including proprietary, open-source offline, and open-source real-time models. Our results show that real-time models generally outperform offline counterparts but still lag behind leading proprietary systems. While scaling model capacity generally yields performance gains, simply increasing the density of sampled input frames does not consistently translate into improved results. These observations suggest inherent limitations in current architectures when handling long-horizon video streams, underscoring the need for models explicitly designed for streaming video processing and analysis.
title: RTV-Bench: Benchmarking MLLM Continuous Perception, Understanding and Reasoning through Real-Time Video

abstract:
With the rise of real-world human-AI interaction applications, such as AI assistants, the need for Streaming Video Dialogue is critical. To address this need, we introduce StreamMind, a video LLM framework that achieves ultra-FPS streaming video processing (100 fps on a single A100) and enables proactive, always-on responses in real time, without explicit user intervention.
To solve the key challenge of the contradiction between linear video streaming speed and quadratic transformer computation cost, we propose a novel perception-cognition interleaving paradigm named ''event-gated LLM invocation'', in contrast to the existing per-time-step LLM invocation. By introducing a Cognition Gate network between the video encoder and the LLM, LLM is only invoked when relevant events occur. To realize the event feature extraction with constant cost, we propose Event-Preserving Feature Extractor (EPFE) based on state-space method, generating a single perception token for spatiotemporal features. These techniques enable the video LLM with full-FPS perception and real-time cognition response.
Experiments on Ego4D and SoccerNet streaming tasks, as well as standard offline benchmarks, demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in both model capability and real-time efficiency, paving the way for ultra-high-FPS applications, such as Game AI and interactive media. The code and data is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Decoupling continuous perception from event-triggered cognition is essential for overcoming the computational mismatch between linear video streams and quadratic LLM processing, enabling ultra-high frame rates.

State Space Models (SSMs) provide an efficient mechanism for aggregating long-term spatiotemporal features with constant computational cost per frame, outperforming attention-based methods for perception.

Initializing a light-weight Cognition Gate with shallow layers of the LLM leverages the LLM's pre-trained world knowledge for accurate event judgment while maintaining real-time decision-making capability.
originalProblem: Existing online video Large Language Models (LLMs) are limited to low frame rates (typically 10-15 FPS), hindering their utility for real-time, fluid human-AI interaction.
The quadratic computational cost of transformer-based LLMs fundamentally conflicts with the linear speed of incoming video streams, creating a bottleneck for continuous processing.
Current streaming VideoLLMs are predominantly reactive, requiring explicit user queries to generate responses, rather than proactively initiating dialogue based on observed events.
results: STREAMMIND achieved unprecedented processing speeds of up to 100 FPS on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU, representing a 10x improvement over previous online video LLMs.

It demonstrated superior performance on streaming video dialogue benchmarks (Ego4D, SoccerNet), with significantly higher Trigger Accuracy (e.g., 43.34% vs. 32.34% on Ego4D) and improved language generation quality (e.g., Fluency 60.2% vs. 45.0%).
The framework also attained state-of-the-art results on traditional offline video understanding benchmarks, including COIN and Ego4D LTA, indicating strong generalizability beyond streaming dialogue.
solution: STREAMMIND introduces an 'event-gated LLM invocation' paradigm that decouples continuous video perception from sparse, event-triggered cognition.

An Event-Preserving Feature Extractor (EPFE), based on a Selective State Space Model (Mamba), processes video frames with constant computational cost to aggregate long-term event-level spatiotemporal context.

A novel Cognition Gate network, initialized using a 'Shallow Layer Transfer' method from the LLM's early layers, determines in real-time whether a relevant event warrants LLM invocation for a proactive response.
paper_summary.summary: STREAMMIND introduces an event-gated cognition framework for streaming video dialogue (StreamingVD) that achieves full frame rate processing by selectively invoking a large language model (LLM) based on detected events. The framework reaches up to 100 FPS on an A100 GPU and shows improved timing alignment and language generation capabilities across various benchmarks.

title: StreamMind: Unlocking Full Frame Rate Streaming Video Dialogue through Event-Gated Cognition

abstract: Unlike offline processing, streaming video vision-language models face two fundamental constraints: causality and accumulation. Causality prevents access to future frames that offline methods exploit, while accumulation causes tokens to grow unbounded, creating efficiency bottlenecks. However, existing approaches only regulate post-LLM kv-cache, leaving costly pre-LLM prefill unchanged. We introduce StreamingTOM, a training-free, plug-and-play two-stage framework that addresses both pre-LLM and post-LLM bottlenecks. Causal Temporal Reduction imposes a fixed per-frame budget and selects tokens based on adjacent-frame changes and token saliency, drastically reducing per-frame prefill cost by processing only a compact subset of visual tokens, ensuring predictable latency. Online Quantized Memory stores tokens in 4-bit format, retrieves relevant groups on demand, and dequantizes them, keeping the active kv-cache bounded regardless of stream length. Experiments demonstrate our method achieves $15.7\times$ kv-cache compression ratio; compared to prior SOTA (LiveVLM), it delivers $1.2\times$ lower peak memory and $2\times$ faster TTFT. StreamingTOM achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among training-free methods with an average of $63.8\%$ on offline benchmarks and $55.8\%$ accuracy and $3.7$ score on RVS. These results demonstrate that real-time streaming video understanding with bounded active memory is achievable without model retraining.

keyInsights: A unified, causally-constrained pipeline can effectively tackle both pre-LLM computational bottlenecks and post-LLM memory accumulation in streaming video understanding.

Adaptive token selection based on a combination of temporal redundancy (similarity to previous frame) and spatial importance (saliency within current frame) allows for efficient, content-aware compression in a causal manner.

Combining 4-bit quantization with selective, query-guided retrieval enables unbounded historical context storage with a bounded active memory footprint, crucial for long-duration reasoning in streaming environments.
originalProblem: Video Large Language Models face unbounded computational cost for pre-LLM visual token processing and linearly growing memory consumption for the post-LLM KV-cache when processing long, continuous video streams.
Existing solutions often rely on global context or future frames, which is incompatible with the strict causality requirement of real-time streaming applications.
Prior training-free methods primarily focus on optimizing post-LLM memory without effectively addressing the substantial pre-LLM computation bottleneck.
results: StreamingTOM achieved a 15.7x kv-cache compression ratio, reducing memory usage for a one-hour video from 18.8 GB to 1.2 GB, and accelerated pre-LLM prefill computation by 3.6x.

The framework delivered state-of-the-art accuracy among streaming systems, with an average of 63.8% on offline benchmarks (VideoMME, MLVU, EgoSchema) and 55.8% accuracy with a 3.7 score on RVS online streaming benchmarks.

It demonstrated predictable latency with sub-0.4s Time-To-First-Token and stable throughput of approximately 20 tokens/s for long sequences, confirming its scalability and practical viability for real-time deployment.
solution: StreamingTOM, a two-stage, training-free framework, combines Causal Temporal Reduction (CTR) for pre-LLM token processing and Online Quantized Memory (OQM) for post-LLM kv-cache management.
CTR uses temporal similarity and spatial saliency signals to causally select a fixed number of most informative tokens per frame before they enter the LLM, reducing pre-LLM computation.

OQM quantizes historical KV pairs to 4-bit and employs question-guided single-pass retrieval to form a bounded active kv-cache, thereby managing memory growth while preserving access to long-horizon context.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Westlake University, The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Zhejiang University, and SII introduced StreamingTOM, a training-free framework designed for efficient video understanding in streaming scenarios. This framework addresses pre-LLM computational costs and post-LLM memory accumulation by implementing causal token reduction and online quantized memory, leading to a 15.7x kv-cache compression and 3.6x pre-LLM prefill acceleration while achieving state-of-the-art accuracy among streaming methods.

title: StreamingTOM: Streaming Token Compression for Efficient Video Understanding

abstract: Online video understanding requires models to perform continuous perception and long-range reasoning within potentially infinite visual streams. Its fundamental challenge lies in the conflict between the unbounded nature of streaming media input and the limited context window of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Current methods primarily rely on passive processing, which often face a trade-off between maintaining long-range context and capturing the fine-grained details necessary for complex tasks. To address this, we introduce EventMemAgent, an active online video agent framework based on a hierarchical memory module. Our framework employs a dual-layer strategy for online videos: short-term memory detects event boundaries and utilizes event-granular reservoir sampling to process streaming video frames within a fixed-length buffer dynamically; long-term memory structuredly archives past observations on an event-by-event basis. Furthermore, we integrate a multi-granular perception toolkit for active, iterative evidence capture and employ Agentic Reinforcement Learning (Agentic RL) to end-to-end internalize reasoning and tool-use strategies into the agent's intrinsic capabilities. Experiments show that EventMemAgent achieves competitive results on online video benchmarks. The code will be released here: this https URL.

organization_info.name: Hangzhou International Innovation Institute, Beihang University

keyInsights: Transitioning from passive to active, agentic information processing is crucial for MLLMs to robustly perform online video understanding by proactively seeking and interpreting relevant information.

An event-centric, hierarchical memory system effectively manages unbounded video streams, preventing information decay and maintaining semantic coherence over long durations with limited input frames.
Agentic Reinforcement Learning allows the agent to internalize complex reasoning and tool-use strategies, making it robust and adaptive without reliance on manual prompt engineering.
originalProblem: Large Multimodal Language Models (MLLMs) are limited by finite context windows, making them unsuitable for continuous, unbounded online video streams.
Existing passive online video processing methods suffer from information decay, significant storage overhead, and lack semantic interpretability for long-range reasoning or fine-grained detail.
Prior agent-based frameworks for video understanding often rely on rudimentary memory modules, limited toolsets, and manual prompt engineering, hindering end-to-end learning of complex strategies.
results: Achieved an average accuracy of 60.75% on the OVO-Bench benchmark, surpassing all existing open-source models and outperforming the proprietary GPT-4o (59.54%).
Delivered an average accuracy of 77.00% on StreamingBench, demonstrating competitive performance in real-time perception tasks with limited input frames (up to 32).

Ablation studies confirmed that the hierarchical memory module, online event segmentation, and specialized perception tools (Object Detection, OCR) are critical for robust understanding and fine-grained detail capture.
solution: A hierarchical memory module, comprising an event-centric Short-Term Memory with online event segmentation and reservoir sampling, and a structured Long-Term Memory for archived events with captions and change logs.
A multi-granular perception toolkit including memory search (temporal and semantic), Optical Character Recognition (OCR), and object detection to actively acquire fine-grained evidence.

An Agentic Reinforcement Learning framework, utilizing the ReAct paradigm and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), to enable end-to-end learning of reasoning paths and adaptive tool-invocation strategies.
paper_summary.summary: EventMemAgent introduces an active agentic framework for online video understanding, overcoming large language model context window limitations with a hierarchical, event-centric memory and adaptive tool use. The system achieved an average accuracy of 60.75% on the OVO-Bench, surpassing GPT-4o, and 77.00% on StreamingBench using only a few input frames.

title: EventMemAgent: Hierarchical Event-Centric Memory for Online Video Understanding with Adaptive Tool Use
