abstract: Recent advances in Streaming Video Understanding has enabled a new interaction paradigm where models respond proactively to user queries. Current proactive VideoLLMs rely on per-frame triggering decision making, which suffers from an efficiency-accuracy dilemma. We propose Em-Garde, a novel framework that decouples semantic understanding from streaming perception. At query time, the Instruction-Guided Proposal Parser transforms user queries into structured, perceptually grounded visual proposals; during streaming, a Lightweight Proposal Matching Module performs efficient embedding-based matching to trigger responses. Experiments on StreamingBench and OVO-Bench demonstrate consistent improvements over prior models in proactive response accuracy and efficiency, validating an effective solution for proactive video understanding under strict computational constraints.

keyInsights: Decoupling high-level semantic reasoning from low-level visual perception significantly resolves the efficiency-accuracy dilemma in proactive video understanding.
Transforming open-ended user queries into structured, temporally localizable, and perceptually groundable visual proposals enables efficient real-time matching by simpler models.
Reinforcement learning, with a carefully designed reward function, effectively optimizes the proposal generation process, leading to more accurate and actionable visual cues.
originalProblem: Existing proactive streaming VideoLLMs face an "efficiency-accuracy dilemma" due to per-frame complex visual perception and semantic reasoning, which is computationally intensive and conflicts with real-time frame rates.
Traditional methods often sacrifice accuracy (by simplifying reasoning) or efficiency (by failing to meet real-time demands) to handle continuous video streams.
A challenge exists in translating open-ended, high-level user queries into reliably detectable visual cues for efficient real-time monitoring.
results: Em-Garde achieved an average F1 score of 30.99% on OVO-Bench (FAR task) and 38.0% accuracy on StreamingBench (PO task), outperforming existing proactive streaming models by substantial margins (e.g., over 3% on StreamingBench compared to the next best model).

The framework demonstrated competitive inference efficiency of approximately 13 FPS, maintaining constant latency and throughput even as video context length increased, unlike other models that degrade.
Ablation studies confirmed that RL training significantly improves proposal quality by shifting them towards temporally aligned and perceptually grounded visual cues, leading to enhanced performance.
solution: Em-Garde introduces a "propose-match" framework that decouples semantic understanding from streaming perception, moving complex reasoning outside the real-time loop.

The Instruction-Guided Proposal Parser (IGPP) processes user queries once at query time, generating perceptually-grounded visual proposals (e.g., "vigorous bubbling") using an MLLM and trained with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning.

The Lightweight Proposal Matching Module (LPMM) continuously runs in the streaming loop, using a VLM-based embedding model to match incoming video segments against these proposals and trigger responses based on similarity changes.
paper_summary.summary: Em-Garde is a propose-match framework that improves proactive streaming video understanding by decoupling complex semantic reasoning from high-frequency visual perception. This approach allows for real-time processing at up to 15 FPS while achieving superior accuracy on proactive response benchmarks like OVO-Bench and StreamingBench.

title: Em-Garde: A Propose-Match Framework for Proactive Streaming Video Understanding

abstract: Recent Omni-multimodal Large Language Models show promise in unified audio, vision, and text modeling. However, streaming audio-video understanding remains challenging, as existing approaches suffer from disjointed capabilities: they typically exhibit incomplete modality support or lack autonomous proactive monitoring. To address this, we present ROMA, a real-time omni-multimodal assistant for unified reactive and proactive interaction. ROMA processes continuous inputs as synchronized multimodal units, aligning dense audio with discrete video frames to handle granularity mismatches. For online decision-making, we introduce a lightweight speak head that decouples response initiation from generation to ensure precise triggering without task conflict. We train ROMA with a curated streaming dataset and a two-stage curriculum that progressively optimizes for streaming format adaptation and proactive responsiveness. To standardize the fragmented evaluation landscape, we reorganize diverse benchmarks into a unified suite covering both proactive (alert, narration) and reactive (QA) settings. Extensive experiments across 12 benchmarks demonstrate ROMA achieves state-of-the-art performance on proactive tasks while competitive in reactive settings, validating its robustness in unified real-time omni-multimodal understanding.

keyInsights: The 'speak head' architecture effectively decouples the timing decision from text generation, which is crucial for precise and robust proactive intervention in streaming scenarios.
A two-stage fine-tuning curriculum, progressively adapting to streaming inputs and then to time-aware decision-making, significantly outperforms direct multi-task training for proactive capabilities.
Aggregating hidden states from multiple upper layers (K=4) of the LLM backbone provides more robust and high-level features for the speak head to make effective streaming decisions.
originalProblem: Existing streaming multimodal models are often limited to either video or speech, lacking unified, synchronized omni-modal support for real-world interaction.
Most current streaming systems are purely reactive, only responding after a query, and lack autonomous proactive monitoring and response capabilities.
Technical challenges include robust cross-modal alignment given heterogeneous temporal granularities of audio and video, and enabling real-time proactive decision-making from stream prefixes.
results: ROMA achieves state-of-the-art performance on proactive alert tasks, with 37.50 on OmniMMI PA for single alerts and 33.81 on OVO-Bench REC for recurring alerts.
The model obtains the best temporal triggering accuracy for real-time narration (35.21 F1 on YouCook2) and the highest GPT-4o scores for narration quality (0.39 on YouCook2).
ROMA outperforms existing streaming VideoLLMs on reactive QA tasks and achieves the best performance among open-source omni-modal models with spoken queries on Video-MME (33.30).
solution: ROMA processes all audio and video signals within one-second intervals as 'multimodal units', aligning features and adapting Time-aligned Multimodal RoPE for continuous causal understanding.

A lightweight 'speak head' (two-layer MLP) is introduced, operating in parallel to the LM head, to autonomously decide when to trigger a response after each unit, decoupling timing from content generation.

A two-stage fine-tuning curriculum adapts a strong foundation model: first for streaming template alignment using reactive QA, then for time-aware decision making with the activated speak head and a joint objective.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the CAS Key Laboratory of AI Safety, ICT, and UCAS introduce ROMA, an open-source omni-multimodal assistant that enables real-time streaming audio-video understanding with unified proactive and reactive interaction. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on proactive alert and narration tasks and competitive results on reactive QA benchmarks, leveraging a novel speak head and two-stage training.

title: ROMA: Real-time Omni-Multimodal Assistant with Interactive Streaming Understanding

abstract: Real-time, continuous understanding of visual signals is essential for real-world interactive AI applications, and poses a fundamental system-level challenge. Existing research on streaming video understanding, however, typically focuses on isolated aspects such as question-answering accuracy under limited visual context or improvements in encoding efficiency, while largely overlooking practical deployability under realistic resource constraints. To bridge this gap, we introduce StreamingEval, a unified evaluation framework for assessing the streaming video understanding capabilities of Video-LLMs under realistic constraints. StreamingEval benchmarks both mainstream offline models and recent online video models under a standardized protocol, explicitly characterizing the trade-off between efficiency, storage and accuracy. Specifically, we adopt a fixed-capacity memory bank to normalize accessible historical visual context, and jointly evaluate visual encoding efficiency, text decoding latency, and task performance to quantify overall system deployability. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets reveal substantial gaps between current Video-LLMs and the requirements of realistic streaming applications, providing a systematic basis for future research in this direction. Codes will be released at this https URL.

keyInsights: High offline accuracy in Video-LLMs does not directly translate to practical usability in real-world streaming applications due to system-level constraints.
There is a systematic trade-off between raw task accuracy, real-time throughput, and latency, highlighting the need for deployability-oriented model design.
Performance is sensitive to historical context capacity and input resolution, with accuracy saturating at moderate memory budgets and declining significantly with reduced visual detail.
originalProblem: Most Video-LLMs are designed and evaluated in conventional offline settings, which differ fundamentally from real-time, continuous streaming applications.
Emerging applications require models to reason with incomplete evolving context, manage persistent visual memory under limited GPU, and meet strict efficiency requirements.
Existing online video understanding benchmarks are often isolated or rely on 'pseudo-streaming,' lacking a unified, system-level evaluation framework for fair comparisons.
results: Most models can sustain a 1 FPS input stream for encoding, but some (e.g., VideoChatOnline) show severe bottlenecks with MaxFPS as low as 0.14.
Mainstream offline Video-LLMs generally achieve higher question-answering accuracy than native online models, but some online models achieve higher overall StreamingScores due to efficiency.
Accuracy tends to saturate around a 1.0G–1.5G memory budget and drops considerably at 0.1G, while 448x448 input resolution consistently yields the highest accuracy for all models.
solution: Introduces StreamingEval, an asynchronous, time-causal evaluation pipeline with decoupled Frame Player, Encoder-and-Memory Updater, and Responder processes.
Implements a 'two settings, one unified budget' strategy with a fixed-capacity memory bank (FIFO eviction) and a byte-level resource budget to standardize historical context across models.
Defines a suite of holistic metrics including MaxFPS (encoding throughput), Time-to-First-Token (TTFT), Memory_bank, and a composite StreamingScore to characterize deployability.
paper_summary.summary: StreamingEval introduces a unified evaluation protocol for Video-LLMs, assessing their deployability in realistic streaming scenarios by jointly considering accuracy, latency, throughput, and memory usage. The framework identifies a substantial performance gap between existing models and the practical requirements of real-time applications under these constraints.

title: StreamingEval: A Unified Evaluation Protocol towards Realistic Streaming Video Understanding

abstract: Despite advancements in Video Large Language Models (Vid-LLMs) improving multimodal understanding, challenges persist in streaming video reasoning due to its reliance on contextual information. Existing paradigms feed all available historical contextual information into Vid-LLMs, resulting in a significant computational burden for visual data processing. Furthermore, the inclusion of irrelevant context distracts models from key details. This paper introduces a challenging task called Context-guided Streaming Video Reasoning (CogStream), which simulates real-world streaming video scenarios, requiring models to identify the most relevant historical contextual information to deduce answers for questions about the current stream. To support CogStream, we present a densely annotated dataset featuring extensive and hierarchical question-answer pairs, generated by a semi-automatic pipeline. Additionally, we present CogReasoner as a baseline model. It effectively tackles this task by leveraging visual stream compression and historical dialogue retrieval. Extensive experiments prove the effectiveness of this method.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

title: CogStream: Context-guided Streaming Video Question Answering

abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled the development of Video-LLMs, advancing multimodal learning by bridging video data with language tasks. However, current video understanding models struggle with processing long video sequences, supporting multi-turn dialogues, and adapting to real-world dynamic scenarios. To address these issues, we propose StreamChat, a training-free framework for streaming video reasoning and conversational interaction. $\StreamChat$ leverages a novel hierarchical memory system to efficiently process and compress video features over extended sequences, enabling real-time, multi-turn dialogue. Our framework incorporates a parallel system scheduling strategy that enhances processing speed and reduces latency, ensuring robust performance in real-world applications. Furthermore, we introduce StreamBench, a versatile benchmark that evaluates streaming video understanding across diverse media types and interactive scenarios, including multi-turn interactions and complex reasoning tasks. Extensive evaluations on StreamBench and other public benchmarks demonstrate that StreamChat significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art models in terms of accuracy and response times, confirming its effectiveness for streaming video understanding. Code is available at StreamChat: this https URL.

keyInsights: Memory compression and efficient management are crucial for real-time performance
Parallel processing threads enable sub-second latency without sacrificing accuracy
Training-free approaches can achieve competitive results through smart architecture design
originalProblem: Current Video-LLM systems struggle with processing long video sequences in real-time
Existing approaches lack effective mechanisms for maintaining context in multi-turn dialogues
No standardized benchmarks exist for evaluating streaming video understanding
results: Achieved 64.7% accuracy on STREAMBENCH, an 8.3% improvement over previous methods
Demonstrated 32 FPS processing speed with sub-second latency (<0.9s)
Showed 2.5% average improvement across existing public benchmarks
Successfully maintained context across long videos and multi-turn conversations
solution: Developed a three-tier hierarchical memory system (long-term, short-term, dialogue)
Implemented parallel system scheduling with selective frame stacking and memory formation
Created STREAMBENCH, a comprehensive evaluation framework for streaming video understanding
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Dalian University and Harvard introduce STREAMCHAT, a groundbreaking training-free framework that enables real-time video understanding and interaction through a novel hierarchical memory system, achieving 64.7% accuracy on streaming tasks while processing video at 32 FPS - a 6x speed improvement over existing approaches.

title: Streaming Video Understanding and Multi-round Interaction with Memory-enhanced Knowledge

abstract:
This paper presents StreamChat, a novel approach that enhances the
interaction capabilities of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) with streaming video
content. In streaming interaction scenarios, existing methods rely solely on
visual information available at the moment a question is posed, resulting in
significant delays as the model remains unaware of subsequent changes in the
streaming video. StreamChat addresses this limitation by innovatively updating
the visual context at each decoding step, ensuring that the model utilizes
up-to-date video content throughout the decoding process. Additionally, we
introduce a flexible and efficient crossattention-based architecture to process
dynamic streaming inputs while maintaining inference efficiency for streaming
interactions. Furthermore, we construct a new dense instruction dataset to
facilitate the training of streaming interaction models, complemented by a
parallel 3D-RoPE mechanism that encodes the relative temporal information of
visual and text tokens. Experimental results demonstrate that StreamChat
achieves competitive performance on established image and video benchmarks and
exhibits superior capabilities in streaming interaction scenarios compared to
state-of-the-art video LMM.

keyInsights: Continuously updating the visual context at each decoding step is critical for LMMs to effectively comprehend and respond to dynamic, real-time video streams.

Specialized architectural components and a cross-attention mechanism are crucial for processing high-frequency visual inputs efficiently and maintaining training stability in streaming multimodal models.
Tailored instruction-tuning datasets, designed to mimic the temporal constraints of streaming interaction, are essential for training LMMs to generate contextually accurate and timely responses.
originalProblem: Existing LMMs for video rely on a static visual context available only at the query's initiation, leading to outdated responses in dynamic streaming scenarios.
This temporal discrepancy causes factual errors and suboptimal user experiences when generating answers during a continuously evolving video stream.
A lack of specialized instruction-tuning datasets that adequately simulate real-time streaming conditions hinders the development of dynamically responsive LMMs.
results: StreamChat-7B achieved equally or more preferable answers in 77% of cases compared to VILA-1.5-40B on a streaming evaluation benchmark, demonstrating superior dynamic interaction despite being a smaller model.

The model shows competitive performance on various image and video benchmarks (e.g., surpassing LLaVA-NeXT-8B by 6.4 points on MMMU), while utilizing a significantly lower number of visual tokens (256 tokens).

Ablation studies validate that all proposed architectural components (V-FFN experts, linear gate) and the dense instruction dataset contribute positively to performance, with the dataset dramatically boosting streaming capabilities.
solution: An architecture featuring cross-attention, Visual Feedforward Network (V-FFN) experts, and a linear gate is proposed for efficient, dynamic visual context updates aligned with the LLM's decoding process.
A parallel 3D-Rotary Positional Embedding (RoPE) extends standard 1D RoPE to ensure precise temporal, height, and width alignment of visual and text tokens.
A novel dense instruction dataset, created from existing dense caption data and an LLM, simulates streaming interaction through heuristic timestamp annotation and attention masking during training.
paper_summary.summary: StreamChat enables Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) to interact with streaming video by continuously updating their visual context during response generation, overcoming the "fixed context" limitation of prior models. It achieves superior real-time performance on streaming benchmarks through a specialized architecture and a novel dense instruction dataset, even outperforming significantly larger LMMs.

title: StreamChat: Chatting with Streaming Video

abstract:
Vision-language models (VLMs) have shown remarkable progress in offline tasks
such as image captioning and video question answering. However, real-time
interactive environments impose new demands on VLMs, requiring them to generate
utterances that are not only semantically accurate but also precisely timed. We
identify two core capabilities necessary for such settings --
$\textit{perceptual updating}$ and $\textit{contingency awareness}$ -- and
propose a new benchmark task, $\textbf{Temporally-Grounded Language Generation
(TGLG)}$, to evaluate them. TGLG requires models to generate utterances in
response to streaming video such that both content and timing align with
dynamic visual input. To support this benchmark, we curate evaluation datasets
from sports broadcasting and egocentric human interaction domains, and
introduce a new metric, $\textbf{TRACE}$, to evaluate TGLG by jointly measuring
semantic similarity and temporal alignment. Finally, we present
$\textbf{Vision-Language Model with Time-Synchronized Interleaving (VLM-TSI)}$,
a model that interleaves visual and linguistic tokens in a time-synchronized
manner, enabling real-time language generation without relying on turn-based
assumptions. Experimental results show that VLM-TSI significantly outperforms a
strong baseline, yet overall performance remains modest -- highlighting the
difficulty of TGLG and motivating further research in real-time VLMs. Code and
data available $\href{this https URL}{here}$.
title: Temporally-Grounded Language Generation: A Benchmark for Real-Time  Vision-Language Models

abstract: Recent progress in video large language models (Video-LLMs) has enabled strong offline reasoning over long and complex videos. However, real-world deployments increasingly require streaming perception and proactive interaction, where video frames arrive online and the system must decide not only what to respond, but also when to respond. In this work, we revisit proactive activation in streaming video as a structured sequence modeling problem, motivated by the observation that temporal transitions in streaming video naturally form span-structured activation patterns. To capture this span-level structure, we model activation signals jointly over a sliding temporal window and update them iteratively as new frames arrive. We propose STRIDE (Structured Temporal Refinement with Iterative DEnoising), which employs a lightweight masked diffusion module at the activation interface to jointly predict and progressively refine activation signals across the window. Extensive experiments on diverse streaming benchmarks and downstream models demonstrate that STRIDE shows more reliable and temporally coherent proactive responses, significantly improving when-to-speak decision quality in online streaming scenarios.
title: STRIDE: When to Speak Meets Sequence Denoising for Streaming Video Understanding

abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive multimodal comprehension capabilities and are being deployed in an increasing number of online video understanding applications. While recent efforts extensively explore advancing VLMs' reasoning power in these cases, deployment constraints are overlooked, leading to overwhelming system overhead in real-world deployments. To address that, we propose Venus, an on-device memory-and-retrieval system for efficient online video understanding. Venus proposes an edge-cloud disaggregated architecture that sinks memory construction and keyframe retrieval from cloud to edge, operating in two stages. In the ingestion stage, Venus continuously processes streaming edge videos via scene segmentation and clustering, where the selected keyframes are embedded with a multimodal embedding model to build a hierarchical memory for efficient storage and retrieval. In the querying stage, Venus indexes incoming queries from memory, and employs a threshold-based progressive sampling algorithm for keyframe selection that enhances diversity and adaptively balances system cost and reasoning accuracy. Our extensive evaluation shows that Venus achieves a 15x-131x speedup in total response latency compared to state-of-the-art methods, enabling real-time responses within seconds while maintaining comparable or even superior reasoning accuracy.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Disaggregating VLM reasoning to the cloud and memory/retrieval to the edge optimally balances computational load and latency, making VLM deployment at the edge practical.
Sparse, hierarchical memory construction at the edge via real-time scene segmentation and clustering enables continuous processing of high-rate video streams on resource-limited devices.
Adaptive, sampling-based, diversity-preserving retrieval ensures comprehensive contextual information for cloud VLMs while minimizing data transfer and inference costs.
originalProblem: Deploying computationally intensive Vision-Language Models (VLMs) efficiently for real-time online video understanding on resource-constrained edge devices presents a major challenge.
Existing cloud-only VLM solutions incur prohibitive communication latency and bandwidth costs when processing continuous video streams.
Prior VLM-based methods focused on algorithmic improvements often overlook deployment constraints, leading to high on-device processing or insufficient information capture.
results: Achieved a 15x–131x speedup in total response latency compared to state-of-the-art baselines, enabling real-time online video understanding within seconds.
Maintained comparable or superior reasoning accuracy across diverse datasets and VLMs, consistently outperforming query-irrelevant baselines.
The Adaptive Keyframe Retrieval (AKR) mechanism reduced VLM inference and communication latency by 1.6x–3.3x on average, and up to 3.8x–7.6x with improved accuracy for specific query types.
solution: A novel edge-cloud disaggregated architecture that offloads memory construction and adaptive keyframe retrieval to edge devices, while reserving computationally heavy VLM reasoning for the cloud.

An ingestion stage processes streaming video using lightweight scene segmentation and clustering for efficient frame filtering, building a hierarchical, sparse memory with multimodal embeddings and auxiliary models at the edge.

A querying stage employs Adaptive Keyframe Retrieval (AKR) with progressive, sampling-based selection to ensure both relevance and diversity of frames, transmitting only the most pertinent data to the cloud VLM.
paper_summary.summary: A system named Venus enables efficient, real-time online video understanding for edge devices by introducing an edge-cloud disaggregated architecture. It offloads memory construction and adaptive keyframe retrieval to the edge, demonstrating a 15x–131x reduction in total response latency while maintaining or improving reasoning accuracy.

title: Venus: An Efficient Edge Memory-and-Retrieval System for VLM-based Online Video Understanding

abstract: Despite significant progress in Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) for offline video understanding, existing online Video-LLMs typically struggle to simultaneously process continuous frame-by-frame inputs and determine optimal response timing, often compromising real-time responsiveness and narrative coherence. To address these limitations, we introduce LiveStar, a pioneering live streaming assistant that achieves always-on proactive responses through adaptive streaming decoding. Specifically, LiveStar incorporates: (1) a training strategy enabling incremental video-language alignment for variable-length video streams, preserving temporal consistency across dynamically evolving frame sequences; (2) a response-silence decoding framework that determines optimal proactive response timing via a single forward pass verification; (3) memory-aware acceleration via peak-end memory compression for online inference on 10+ minute videos, combined with streaming key-value cache to achieve 1.53x faster inference. We also construct an OmniStar dataset, a comprehensive dataset for training and benchmarking that encompasses 15 diverse real-world scenarios and 5 evaluation tasks for online video understanding. Extensive experiments across three benchmarks demonstrate LiveStar's state-of-the-art performance, achieving an average 19.5% improvement in semantic correctness with 18.1% reduced timing difference compared to existing online Video-LLMs, while improving FPS by 12.0% across all five OmniStar tasks. Our model and dataset can be accessed at this https URL.

keyInsights: A dynamic response-silence paradigm (SCAM + SVeD) effectively overcomes the limitations of EOS-token based methods, leading to more robust and temporally consistent online video understanding.
Intelligent memory compression (Peak-End Memory Compression) and optimized KV caching are crucial for achieving efficient, low-latency processing of long, continuous video streams.
Developing comprehensive, multi-scenario, multi-task datasets like OmniStar is essential for training and holistically evaluating online video understanding models against real-world complexities.
originalProblem: Existing online Video-LLMs rely heavily on End-Of-Sequence (EOS) tokens for silence, leading to imbalanced training, consecutive frame inconsistency, and pre-training misalignment that degrades core visual-language understanding.
EOS tokens can cause "vocabulary confusion" by frequently appearing in responses, diluting semantic coherence and introducing ambiguity.

Current benchmarks for online video understanding are limited in scope, focusing on narrow scenarios or single tasks, which fails to encompass the diversity and complexity of real-world live streaming applications.
results: LiveStar achieves an average 19.5% increase in Semantic Correctness (SemCor) and 18.1% reduction in Timing Difference (TimDiff) compared to the second-best model on the OmniStar online benchmark.
The system demonstrates a 12.0% increase in Frames Per Second (FPS) on OmniStar, showcasing improved inference efficiency for real-time applications.

On the Ego4D Narration Stream benchmark, LiveStar obtained the lowest Perplexity (1.97) and TimeDiff (1.76), with the highest Token Accuracy (61.1%), marking an 8.7% improvement over the second-best LION-FS.
solution: A novel response-silence paradigm featuring Streaming Causal Attention Masks (SCAM) for training and Streaming Verification Decoding (SVeD) for dynamic, perplexity-based inference, eliminating reliance on EOS tokens.
Efficient long-term video processing through Peak-End Memory Compression and a dual-level Streaming Key-Value Cache, enabling handling of extended video contexts.

Introduction of OmniStar, a comprehensive multi-scenario, multi-task dataset with 20,137 expert-annotated video streams and five online evaluation tasks for robust online video understanding evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: LiveStar, developed by researchers from CASIA and Kuaishou Technology, integrates adaptive streaming decoding and a novel response-silence paradigm for real-time online video understanding. The system achieves an average 19.5% increase in Semantic Correctness and 18.1% reduction in Timing Difference on the OmniStar benchmark, while boosting inference speed by 12.0% FPS.

title: LiveStar: Live Streaming Assistant for Real-World Online Video Understanding

abstract: Streaming video question answering (Streaming Video QA) poses distinct challenges for multimodal large language models (MLLMs), as video frames arrive sequentially and user queries can be issued at arbitrary time points. Existing solutions relying on fixed-size memory or naive compression often suffer from context loss or memory overflow, limiting their effectiveness in long-form, real-time scenarios. We present Vista, a novel framework for scene-aware streaming video QA that enables efficient and scalable reasoning over continuous video streams. The innovation of Vista can be summarized in three aspects: (1) scene-aware segmentation, where Vista dynamically clusters incoming frames into temporally and visually coherent scene units; (2) scene-aware compression, where each scene is compressed into a compact token representation and stored in GPU memory for efficient index-based retrieval, while full-resolution frames are offloaded to CPU memory; and (3) scene-aware recall, where relevant scenes are selectively recalled and reintegrated into the model input upon receiving a query, enabling both efficiency and completeness. Vista is model-agnostic and integrates seamlessly with a variety of vision-language backbones, enabling long-context reasoning without compromising latency or memory efficiency. Extensive experiments on StreamingBench demonstrate that Vista achieves state-of-the-art performance, establishing a strong baseline for real-world streaming video understanding.

keyInsights: Leveraging semantically coherent 'scene units' rather than individual frames allows for more robust retrieval and efficient long-context reasoning in streaming video.
An online, query-agnostic approach combining dynamic scene segmentation, compression, and selective recall effectively balances the competing demands of relevance, efficiency, and responsiveness.
This scene-aware methodology enables MLLMs to process continuous video streams under severe resource constraints while preserving crucial information for accurate post-hoc query answering.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with real-time, streaming video due to unbounded input length and strict low-latency requirements for interactive applications.
Handling post-hoc queries, where user questions arrive at arbitrary times, prevents pre-emptive frame importance estimation and necessitates query-agnostic processing during streaming.
GPU memory constraints make it infeasible to retain all high-resolution frames, demanding efficient memory management and compression.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on StreamingBench, with a +29.60% absolute improvement in Multimodal Alignment over the LLaVA-OneVision-7B baseline, also outperforming GPT-4o.
Maintained consistently low GPU memory usage and stable inference latency (Time-To-First-Token) as input frames increased, contrasting with 'All Frames' strategies that incur Out-Of-Memory errors.
Demonstrated robustness and versatility by achieving 63.8% on MLVU and 58.7% on EgoSchema, surpassing strong offline MLLMs and leading streaming models on long-video QA benchmarks.
solution: Vista employs an online, unsupervised scene-aware segmentation method to dynamically group incoming video frames into semantically coherent scene units based on visual similarity.
A scene-aware compression strategy applies temporal and spatial compression to completed scenes, offloading full features and retaining compact scene tokens in fast memory.
A query-driven scene-aware recall mechanism efficiently retrieves the most relevant full-resolution frames from stored scenes and recent local frames upon query arrival to feed the MLLM.
paper_summary.summary: The Vista framework, developed by researchers from Huazhong University of Science and Technology and Ping An Technology, introduces a scene-aware optimization strategy to enable efficient and scalable streaming video question answering for MLLMs. It achieved state-of-the-art performance on StreamingBench, demonstrating a +29.60% absolute improvement in Multimodal Alignment while maintaining low GPU memory and stable latency.

title: Vista: Scene-Aware Optimization for Streaming Video Question Answering under Post-Hoc Queries

abstract: Understanding long videos with multimodal large language models (MLLMs) remains challenging due to the heavy redundancy across frames and the need for temporally coherent representations. Existing static strategies, such as sparse sampling, frame compression, and clustering, are optimized for offline settings and often produce fragmented or over-compressed outputs when applied to continuous video streams. We present VideoScaffold, a dynamic representation framework designed for streaming video understanding. It adaptively adjusts event granularity according to video duration while preserving fine-grained visual semantics. VideoScaffold introduces two key components: Elastic-Scale Event Segmentation (EES), which performs prediction-guided segmentation to dynamically refine event boundaries, and Hierarchical Event Consolidation (HEC), which progressively aggregates semantically related segments into multi-level abstractions. Working in concert, EES and HEC enable VideoScaffold to transition smoothly from fine-grained frame understanding to abstract event reasoning as the video stream unfolds. Extensive experiments across both offline and streaming video understanding benchmarks demonstrate that VideoScaffold achieves state-of-the-art performance. The framework is modular and plug-and-play, seamlessly extending existing image-based MLLMs to continuous video comprehension. The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Dynamically adjusting event granularity based on prediction errors (Elastic-Scale Event Segmentation) enables efficient and semantically coherent processing of continuous video streams, inspired by human cognition.
A multi-level visual hierarchy, from fine-grained frame abstraction to higher-level event consolidation, effectively manages redundancy and captures long-term temporal dependencies in videos.
Operating under causal constraints, where decisions are based only on past and current information, is crucial for extending MLLMs to real-time, streaming video understanding applications.
originalProblem: Existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) struggle with the redundancy and computational cost of long videos, often relying on static sampling that loses crucial details.
Most video MLLMs are designed for "offline" processing, failing to meet the real-time, causal constraints of continuous video streams where future frames are unavailable.
Current streaming approaches often use fixed-length frame chunks, leading to fragmented event boundaries or over-compressed representations that lack dynamic adaptation to evolving content.
results: VideoScaffold achieved state-of-the-art performance on open-ended short-video QA benchmarks, surpassing larger 13B models with a smaller 7B language backbone.

On long-video understanding tasks (e.g., LV-Bench, MLVU), it outperformed all other 7B-scale baselines and delivered results comparable to MLLMs utilizing significantly larger 70B models or processing far more frames.

The framework secured the highest average performance on StreamingBench, a benchmark for real-time visual comprehension, among existing streaming MLLMs, validating its efficacy under causal streaming constraints.
solution: VideoScaffold introduces Elastic-Scale Event Segmentation (EES) that dynamically segments video into multi-granularity events using prediction errors across hierarchical temporal levels.

Hierarchical Event Consolidation (HEC) aggregates visual embeddings within each event segment by identifying "essential tokens" (frames with highest prediction error) and employing cross-attention, creating compact and semantically rich representations.
The framework is designed as a modular, plug-and-play component that seamlessly integrates with existing image-based MLLMs (e.g., LLaVA) to extend their capabilities to continuous video streams.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from USTC and Ant Group developed VideoScaffold, a framework that processes continuous video streams for multimodal large language models by dynamically segmenting events and consolidating information hierarchically. The method enables real-time, causal video understanding while achieving state-of-the-art results on streaming benchmarks and improving performance on various offline video tasks.

title: VideoScaffold: Elastic-Scale Visual Hierarchies for Streaming Video Understanding in MLLMs

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong capabilities in image understanding, motivating recent efforts to extend them to video reasoning. However, existing Video LLMs struggle in online streaming scenarios, where long temporal context must be preserved under strict memory constraints. We propose WAT (Watching Before Thinking), a two-stage framework for online video reasoning. WAT separates processing into a query-independent watching stage and a query-triggered thinking stage. The watching stage builds a hierarchical memory system with a Short-Term Memory (STM) that buffers recent frames and a fixed-capacity Long-Term Memory (LTM) that maintains a diverse summary of historical content using a redundancy-aware eviction policy. In the thinking stage, a context-aware retrieval mechanism combines the query with the current STM context to retrieve relevant historical frames from the LTM for cross-temporal reasoning. To support training for online video tasks, we introduce WAT-85K, a dataset containing streaming-style annotations emphasizing real-time perception, backward tracing, and forecasting. Experiments show that WAT achieves state-of-the-art performance on online video benchmarks, including 77.7% accuracy on StreamingBench and 55.2% on OVO-Bench, outperforming existing open-source online Video LLMs while operating at real-time frame rates.

keyInsights: Decoupling continuous visual perception from query-driven reasoning effectively addresses memory efficiency and temporal coherence challenges in online video understanding.

A hierarchical memory system combining short-term fine-grained details and long-term diverse summaries, managed by a redundancy-aware eviction policy, maintains comprehensive context over extended video streams.
Explicitly aligning query representations with retrieved visual evidence through a contrastive learning objective (RACL) enhances the accuracy and relevance of long-term memory retrieval.
originalProblem: Existing MLLMs for video understanding are primarily designed for offline processing, struggling with continuous, real-time streaming data.
Online video understanding faces a fundamental challenge in preserving both fine-grained temporal context and long-term semantic diversity under strict memory and computational constraints.
Prior online Video LLMs rely on reactive, query-driven memory updates, which can lead to the premature discarding of important visual information before it can be queried.
results: WAT achieved state-of-the-art performance on online video benchmarks, scoring 77.70% overall accuracy on StreamingBench and 55.2% on OVO-Bench among open-source models.
The framework surpassed proprietary MLLMs such as Gemini 1.5 Pro (75.69%) and GPT-4o (73.28%) on StreamingBench, operating under real-time (1 FPS) constraints.
Ablation studies confirmed that the combined hierarchical memory (STM + LTM) and the Retrieval Alignment Contrastive Learning (RACL) loss significantly improved performance on both online benchmarks.
solution: A two-stage framework, WAT (Watching Before Thinking), decouples query-independent long-term visual perception (watching) from query-triggered reasoning (thinking).

A hierarchical memory system is introduced, consisting of a Short-Term Memory (STM) for high-fidelity recent frames and a Long-Term Memory (LTM) with a redundancy-aware eviction policy for diverse historical context.

A context-aware retrieval mechanism, enhanced by Retrieval Alignment Contrastive Learning (RACL), fuses queries with STM context to retrieve relevant LTM features for reasoning, and a new dataset (WAT-85K) was created to facilitate training.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Xi’an Jiaotong University and China Telecom developed WAT, a two-stage framework for online video understanding that separates continuous visual perception from query-driven reasoning. The framework, featuring a hierarchical memory system, achieves 77.70% accuracy on StreamingBench and 55.2% on OVO-Bench, outperforming prior open-source and some proprietary models.

title: WAT: Online Video Understanding Needs Watching Before Thinking

abstract: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) excel at understanding videos in-context, provided they have full access to the video when answering queries. However, these models face challenges in streaming scenarios where hour-long videos must be processed online, and questions need timely responses. In this work, we propose a training-free approach compatible with standard Video-LLMs, leveraging three key concepts: 1) LLM-informed selection of visual tokens to identify those that the LLM has attended to and contributed to its understanding of each short clip. Our attention-based selection allows us to discard up to ~95% of unimportant visual tokens with minimal performance loss; 2) Recurrent processing of past selected tokens to generate temporally coherent understanding of each processed clip; 3) Caption-based question answering for lightweight and accurate responses. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on streaming video benchmarks, striking a balance between efficiency and effectiveness.

keyInsights: LLM's internal attention mechanisms can effectively guide visual token selection, enabling aggressive compression (up to 95% token discard) with minimal impact on performance.
A recurrent memory (FIFO queue) of selected visual tokens is crucial for maintaining temporal coherence and improving performance in long video understanding tasks.

LLMs demonstrate superior performance in answering questions by reasoning over retrieved textual captions rather than direct visual tokens, aligning with their proficiency in extended textual contexts.
originalProblem: Current Video-LLMs face prohibitive computational costs and context length limitations when processing long or streaming videos.
Dense frame sampling to avoid information loss leads to an unmanageable number of visual tokens and high latency.
Existing solutions often lack temporal coherence for continuous understanding or suffer from high memory consumption in streaming scenarios.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art accuracy on streaming video question answering benchmarks (e.g., 65.3% on RVS-Ego and 57.7% on RVS-Movie), surpassing previous methods like ReKV.
Demonstrated significant efficiency improvements, reducing VRAM usage by 11GB (25GB vs 36GB) and latency by nearly 1 second (1.9s vs 2.7s) compared to prior solutions.
Exhibited model-agnosticism and robust performance even with aggressive visual compression (e.g., 6% token retention nearly matched full model performance on NextQA-valset).
solution: LLM-informed Visual Token Selection: Leverages LLM cross-attention scores to identify and retain only the most important visual tokens, achieving high compression rates (e.g., 95%).
Long Video Recurrent Processing: Implements a recurrent mechanism using a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) queue for selected visual tokens to maintain temporal context across sequential video clips.

Efficient Video Question Answering: Stores auto-generated textual captions for each clip and retrieves top-K relevant captions using Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) to answer user queries through the LLM.
paper_summary.summary: A training-free and model-agnostic method for efficient streaming video-LLMs from researchers at Toyota Motor Europe, the University of Oxford, and Athena RC, named rLiVS, significantly improves video question answering. It intelligently selects visual tokens using LLM attention, maintains temporal context with recurrent memory, and retrieves textual captions for answering, achieving state-of-the-art performance on streaming video QA benchmarks with reduced memory usage and latency.

title: Recurrent Attention-based Token Selection for Efficient Streaming Video-LLMs

abstract: We present LongLive, a frame-level autoregressive (AR) framework for real-time and interactive long video generation. Long video generation presents challenges in both efficiency and quality. Diffusion and Diffusion-Forcing models can produce high-quality videos but suffer from low efficiency due to bidirectional attention. Causal attention AR models support KV caching for faster inference, but often degrade in quality on long videos due to memory challenges during long-video training. In addition, beyond static prompt-based generation, interactive capabilities, such as streaming prompt inputs, are critical for dynamic content creation, enabling users to guide narratives in real time. This interactive requirement significantly increases complexity, especially in ensuring visual consistency and semantic coherence during prompt transitions. To address these challenges, LongLive adopts a causal, frame-level AR design that integrates a KV-recache mechanism that refreshes cached states with new prompts for smooth, adherent switches; streaming long tuning to enable long video training and to align training and inference (train-long-test-long); and short window attention paired with a frame-level attention sink, shorten as frame sink, preserving long-range consistency while enabling faster generation. With these key designs, LongLive fine-tunes a 1.3B-parameter short-clip model to minute-long generation in just 32 GPU-days. At inference, LongLive sustains 20.7 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100, achieves strong performance on VBench in both short and long videos. LongLive supports up to 240-second videos on a single H100 GPU. LongLive further supports INT8-quantized inference with only marginal quality loss.
title: LongLive: Real-time Interactive Long Video Generation

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models excel at offline audio-visual understanding, but their ability to serve as mobile assistants in continuous real-world streams remains underexplored. In daily phone use, mobile assistants must track streaming audio-visual inputs and respond at the right time, yet existing benchmarks are often restricted to multiple-choice questions or use shorter videos. In this paper, we introduce PhoStream, the first mobile-centric streaming benchmark that unifies on-screen and off-screen scenarios to evaluate video, audio, and temporal reasoning. PhoStream contains 5,572 open-ended QA pairs from 578 videos across 4 scenarios and 10 capabilities. We build it with an Automated Generative Pipeline backed by rigorous human verification, and evaluate models using a realistic Online Inference Pipeline and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation for open-ended responses. Experiments reveal a temporal asymmetry in LLM-judged scores (0-100): models perform well on Instant and Backward tasks (Gemini 3 Pro exceeds 80), but drop sharply on Forward tasks (16.40), largely due to early responses before the required visual and audio cues appear. This highlights a fundamental limitation: current MLLMs struggle to decide when to speak, not just what to say. Code and datasets used in this work will be made publicly accessible at this https URL.

keyInsights: MLLMs exhibit a fundamental "Early Response" bias, demonstrating difficulty in deferring responses and frequently answering prematurely for future-oriented (Forward) tasks, even when necessary information has not yet appeared in the stream.

The decision of *when* to speak is a critical, yet underdeveloped, aspect of intelligence for proactive mobile AI assistants, highlighting a gap in current MLLMs' temporal prudence and sophisticated reasoning capabilities.

Incorporating audio modality, while generally improving immediate (Instant) and recall-based (Backward) tasks, can sometimes slightly decrease scores and increase the early response rate for forward-looking tasks, suggesting complex interactions between multimodal perception and temporal decision-making.
originalProblem: Current Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) excel at offline audio-visual understanding but demonstrate inadequate performance in continuous, real-time streaming scenarios essential for mobile AI assistants.

Existing streaming benchmarks often lack comprehensive coverage of diverse mobile-centric interactions (both on-screen and off-screen), utilize short video durations, employ restrictive question formats, and overlook the critical aspect of an assistant's ability to decide *when* to respond.
Developing a scalable and verifiable method for generating high-quality, long-duration, temporally-constrained video QA data for complex MLLM evaluation remains a challenge.
results: Models show a substantial performance disparity across temporal tasks, with a sharp drop on Forward tasks (e.g., Gemini 3 Pro scoring 16.40) compared to Instant (80.83) and Backward (82.19) tasks.

High "Early Response" (ER) rates are identified as the predominant failure mode for Forward tasks, with proprietary models like Gemini 3 Pro showing 79.12% ER and Qwen3-Omni-30B-A3B reaching 97.89% ER.

While proprietary models generally achieve higher overall scores than open-source counterparts, even the top-performing models significantly struggle with Forward tasks and consistently exhibit considerable early response tendencies.
solution: PhoStream comprises 5,572 open-ended QA pairs derived from 578 long mobile-centric videos (average 13.3 minutes) covering YouTube Vlogs, Phone Tutorials, Phone Records, and EgoBlind datasets, ensuring diverse on-screen and off-screen mobile interactions.

A novel task definition categorizes questions into three temporal scopes (Backward, Instant, Forward) with strict timestamp cutoffs to control evidence availability, generated through an automated Gemini 3 Pro pipeline followed by two rounds of human verification.

A strict online inference environment simulates real mobile streaming, processing sequential video/audio inputs with a sliding memory window, and requires models to autonomously decide *when* to provide a response, evaluated using an LLM-as-a-Judge framework.
paper_summary.summary: PhoStream introduces the first mobile-centric, omnimodal streaming benchmark for AI assistants, featuring long videos and open-ended questions, to evaluate real-world performance. Experiments using PhoStream demonstrate that current Multimodal Large Language Models significantly struggle with dynamic temporal reasoning, exhibiting a strong "Early Response" bias when deciding when to respond to future-oriented queries.

title: PhoStream: Benchmarking Real-World Streaming for Omnimodal Assistants in Mobile Scenarios

abstract: Audio-visual event parsing plays a crucial role in understanding multimodal video content, but existing methods typically rely on offline processing of entire videos with huge model sizes, limiting their real-time applicability. We introduce Online Audio-Visual Event Parsing (On-AVEP), a novel paradigm for parsing audio, visual, and audio-visual events by sequentially analyzing incoming video streams. The On-AVEP task necessitates models with two key capabilities: (1) Accurate online inference, to effectively distinguish events with unclear and limited context in online settings, and (2) Real-time efficiency, to balance high performance with computational constraints. To cultivate these, we propose the Predictive Future Modeling (PreFM) framework featured by (a) predictive multimodal future modeling to infer and integrate beneficial future audio-visual cues, thereby enhancing contextual understanding and (b) modality-agnostic robust representation along with focal temporal prioritization to improve precision and generalization. Extensive experiments on the UnAV-100 and LLP datasets show PreFM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods by a large margin with significantly fewer parameters, offering an insightful approach for real-time multimodal video understanding. Code is available at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Visual Intelligence + X International Joint Laboratory

keyInsights: Dynamically generating and integrating pseudo-future contextual cues significantly enhances the accuracy of online multimodal event parsing, effectively addressing the limitation of missing future context.
Leveraging knowledge distillation from powerful pre-trained teacher models to create modality-agnostic representations leads to more robust and generalizable event features for online processing.
Applying focal temporal prioritization and random segment sampling during training improves the model's ability to adapt to dynamic scene variations and accurately predict events in real-time.
originalProblem: Existing audio-visual event parsing (AVEP) methods operate offline, analyzing entire video sequences, which limits their applicability in real-time scenarios.
Current AVEP approaches have large model sizes and high computational costs, making them unsuitable for deployment on resource-constrained devices like wearable devices or in autonomous driving.
Online event parsing faces the challenge of accurately predicting ongoing events with only historical and current multimodal information, lacking future context.
results: PreFM achieved substantial performance gains on On-AVEL (UnAV-100) and On-AVVP (LLP) tasks, with PreFM+ surpassing the second-best method by +8.3 mAP and +14.5 Avg F1-score on UnAV-100.

The framework demonstrated high computational efficiency, utilizing only 2.7% of parameters, 0.6% of FLOPs, and 4.8% of peak memory compared to the next best method on On-AVEL, while achieving 51.9 FPS.

Ablation studies confirmed that the Pseudo-Future mechanism (+1.6% Avg F1 over baseline), Modality-agnostic Robust Representation (+3.4%), and Focal Temporal Prioritization were crucial for the observed performance improvements, with the full PreFM model achieving a +5.5% improvement over the baseline.
solution: The Predictive Future Modeling (PreFM) framework was introduced for Online Audio-Visual Event Parsing (On-AVEP), designed for efficiency and accuracy in real-time streaming data.
A Predictive Multimodal Future Modeling (PMFM) network dynamically generates pseudo-future multimodal sequences to compensate for the inherent lack of future information in online settings.

The framework incorporates Modality-agnostic Robust Representation (MRR) using pre-trained teacher models and Focal Temporal Prioritization to refine current representations and emphasize predictions closer to the current moment.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Beijing Jiaotong University developed PreFM, an online audio-visual event parsing framework that processes streaming multimodal data by predicting future context. This method achieved superior accuracy compared to offline state-of-the-art methods, with an +8.3 mAP and +14.5 Avg F1-score improvement on UnAV-100, while operating with significantly reduced computational resources, using only 2.7% of parameters and 0.6% of FLOPs.

title: PreFM: Online Audio-Visual Event Parsing via Predictive Future Modeling

abstract: Despite the significant advancements of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) on established benchmarks, there remains a notable gap in suitable evaluation regarding their applicability in the emerging domain of long-context streaming video understanding. Current benchmarks for video understanding typically emphasize isolated single-instance text inputs and fail to evaluate the capacity to sustain temporal reasoning throughout the entire duration of video streams. To address these limitations, we introduce SVBench, a pioneering benchmark with temporal multi-turn question-answering chains specifically designed to thoroughly assess the capabilities of streaming video understanding of current LVLMs. We design a semi-automated annotation pipeline to obtain 49,979 Question-Answer (QA) pairs of 1,353 streaming videos, which includes generating QA chains that represent a series of consecutive multi-turn dialogues over video segments and constructing temporal linkages between successive QA chains. Our experimental results, obtained from 14 models in dialogue and streaming evaluations, reveal that while the closed-source GPT-4o outperforms others, most open-source LVLMs struggle with long-context streaming video understanding. We also construct a StreamingChat model, which significantly outperforms open-source LVLMs on our SVBench and achieves comparable performance on diverse vision-language benchmarks. We expect SVBench to advance the research of streaming video understanding by providing a comprehensive and in-depth analysis of current LVLMs. Our benchmark and model can be accessed at this https URL.

keyInsights: Even state-of-the-art closed-source LVLMs like GPT-4o achieve a maximum overall score of 66.29, significantly lagging human performance, indicating a substantial gap in current models' streaming video understanding capabilities.

"Streaming evaluation" consistently yields lower scores than "dialogue evaluation" across all models, highlighting the added complexity and challenges posed by dynamic temporal dependencies in continuous streaming content.

LVLMs generally perform better on skills like Intention Inference and Potentiality Assessment but struggle with complex cognitive tasks such as Counterfactual Reasoning and Spatio-Temporal Speculation.
originalProblem: Existing video understanding benchmarks utilize short clips, isolated text inputs, and static multi-turn dialogues, failing to assess continuous temporal reasoning in dynamic streaming contexts.

Current Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) lack suitable evaluation methods to gauge their ability to handle long-context streaming videos, where dialogues evolve concurrently with the visual stream without foreknowledge of future content.
A comprehensive benchmark was missing to evaluate LVLMs' capacity for sustained temporal reasoning and coherent multi-turn dialogues across extensive, dynamic video streams.
results: GPT-4o achieved the highest overall scores (66.29 in dialogue, 58.17 in streaming), leading all models, while open-source LVLMs showed significantly lower performance, underscoring the challenge of the SVBENCH task.

The proposed StreamingChat baseline model achieved overall scores of 59.41 (dialogue) and 53.90 (streaming), outperforming the next best open-source LVLM (MiniCPM-V 2.6) by 9.41% and 3.30% respectively.

Ablation studies confirmed that models benefit from multi-turn dialogue context, with improved METEOR and GPT4-Score metrics when previous QAs are provided, demonstrating the value of dialogue evaluation.
solution: Researchers developed SVBENCH, a novel benchmark comprising 1,353 high-quality, long streaming videos and 49,979 temporal multi-turn dialogue QA chains, generated via a semi-automated, LLM-assisted pipeline with extensive manual annotation.

The benchmark defines a new task: Temporal Multi-Turn Dialogue for Streaming Videos, which requires LVLMs to engage in contextually linked conversations that evolve in sync with the video flow, addressing both within-clip QA and cross-clip temporal linkages.

They also introduced StreamingChat, an InternVL2-based LVLM, specifically fine-tuned on SVBENCH data to serve as a strong open-source baseline model, demonstrating how specialized training can improve performance in this new task.
paper_summary.summary: SVBENCH introduces the first benchmark for streaming video understanding, featuring temporal multi-turn dialogues derived from long videos. It evaluates Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) across nine video understanding skills, demonstrating that current models, including the proposed StreamingChat baseline, still significantly underperform human capabilities.

title: SVBench: A Benchmark with Temporal Multi-Turn Dialogues for Streaming Video Understanding

abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) could power real-time assistants and autonomous agents, but they face a critical challenge: understanding near-infinite video streams without escalating latency and memory usage. Processing entire videos with full attention leads to quadratic computational costs and poor performance on long videos. Meanwhile, simple sliding window methods are also flawed, as they either break coherence or suffer from high latency due to redundant recomputation. In this paper, we introduce StreamingVLM, a model designed for real-time, stable understanding of infinite visual input. Our approach is a unified framework that aligns training with streaming inference. During inference, we maintain a compact KV cache by reusing states of attention sinks, a short window of recent vision tokens, and a long window of recent text tokens. This streaming ability is instilled via a simple supervised fine-tuning (SFT) strategy that applies full attention on short, overlapped video chunks, which effectively mimics the inference-time attention pattern without training on prohibitively long contexts. For evaluation, we build Inf-Streams-Eval, a new benchmark with videos averaging over two hours that requires dense, per-second alignment between frames and text. On Inf-Streams-Eval, StreamingVLM achieves a 66.18% win rate against GPT-4O mini and maintains stable, real-time performance at up to 8 FPS on a single NVIDIA H100. Notably, our SFT strategy also enhances general VQA abilities without any VQA-specific fine-tuning, improving performance on LongVideoBench by +4.30 and OVOBench Realtime by +5.96. Code is available at this https URL.
title: StreamingVLM: Real-Time Understanding for Infinite Video Streams

abstract: We study quantitative isoperimetric inequalities for two different perimeter-type functionals. We first consider classical capillarity functionals, which measure the perimeter of sets in a Euclidean half-space, assigning a constant weight $\lambda \in (-1, 1)$ to the portion of the boundary that lies on the boundary of the half-space. We then consider the relative perimeter of sets contained in some suitable convex cone in the Euclidean space. In both settings, we establish sharp quantitative isoperimetric inequalities in the so-called strong form. More precisely, we show that the isoperimetric deficit of a competitor not only controls the Fraenkel asymmetry, but it also controls an oscillation asymmetry that measures how much the unit normals to the boundary of a competitor deviate from those of an isoperimetric set. Our technique is also able to explicitly identify a center that can be employed to compute the asymmetries. In particular, we also derive barycentric versions of these quantitative isoperimetric inequalities (both in the classical and in the strong form). The proofs are based on the derivation of Fuglede-type estimates for graphs defined on general spherically convex domains, in combination with a new application of the selection principle that directly provides the inequalities in barycentric form.
title: Quantitative isoperimetric inequalities in capillarity problems and cones in strong and barycentric forms

abstract: Real-time understanding of continuous video streams is essential for intelligent agents operating in high-stakes environments, including autonomous vehicles, surveillance drones, and disaster response robots. Yet, most existing video understanding and highlight detection methods assume access to the entire video during inference, making them unsuitable for online or streaming scenarios. In particular, current models optimize for offline summarization, failing to support step-by-step reasoning needed for real-time decision-making. We introduce Aha, an autoregressive highlight detection framework that predicts the relevance of each video frame against a task described in natural language. Without accessing future video frames, Aha utilizes a multimodal vision-language model and lightweight, decoupled heads trained on a large, curated dataset of human-centric video labels. To enable scalability, we introduce the Dynamic SinkCache mechanism that achieves constant memory usage across infinite-length streams without degrading performance on standard benchmarks. This encourages the hidden representation to capture high-level task objectives, enabling effective frame-level rankings for informativeness, relevance, and uncertainty with respect to the natural language task. Aha achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on highlight detection benchmarks, surpassing even prior offline, full-context approaches and video-language models by +5.9% on TVSum and +8.3% on Mr. Hisum in mAP (mean Average Precision). We explore Aha's potential for real-world robotics applications given a task-oriented natural language input and a continuous, robot-centric video. Both experiments demonstrate Aha's potential effectiveness as a real-time reasoning module for downstream planning and long-horizon understanding.

keyInsights: An autoregressive architecture, combined with task-aware prediction heads, can achieve state-of-the-art online highlight detection, even surpassing many traditional offline methods on standard benchmarks.

Dynamically configuring the transformer's memory sink with task objective tokens (Dynamic SinkCache) is an optimal strategy for maintaining constant memory usage and long-range semantic grounding in continuous video streams.
Explicitly modeling probabilistic uncertainty in frame-level highlight predictions provides crucial confidence indicators for decision-making in dynamic, partially observable environments.
originalProblem: Most contemporary video understanding and highlight detection (HD) methods operate offline, requiring access to the entire video, which makes them unsuitable for real-time streaming applications.

Existing streaming video analysis approaches, including some Video-LLMs, often treat HD as an auxiliary task or rely on non-causal smoothing, leading to suboptimal performance and violating strict online constraints.

There is a need for robust, truly online, task-conditioned highlight detection that can perform on standard benchmarks for intelligent agents operating in high-stakes environments requiring immediate, causal decision-making.
results: AHA achieved state-of-the-art performance on TVSum (93.0 top-5 mAP with domain adaptation, 91.6 zero-shot) and Mr.Hisum (64.19 mAP@50), outperforming prior online and numerous offline methods.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of language conditioning (11.8-point drop without it) and demonstrated that the Dynamic SinkCache mechanism significantly surpassed other memory strategies.

The framework exhibited robustness to various simulated video quality degradations, maintaining strong performance with only marginal drops, and showed practical applicability on long-form, noisy robotics footage from the SCOUT dataset.
solution: AHA introduces an autoregressive, decoder-only transformer framework with lightweight, decoupled prediction heads for relevance, informativeness, and uncertainty, processing frames one at a time.

It implements a novel Dynamic SinkCache mechanism that dynamically constructs its sink to contain task objective tokens, ensuring constant memory usage for arbitrarily long video streams without performance degradation.

A multi-objective training strategy is employed, including video quality dropout for robustness and an auxiliary language modeling head for enriched semantic representations, using the new Human Intuition Highlight Dataset (HIHD) for large-scale, task-conditioned OHD.
paper_summary.summary: AHA (Autoregressive Highlight Detection) presents a framework for online, task-conditioned highlight detection in continuous video streams that operates strictly without access to future frames. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks like TVSum (93.0 top-5 mAP with adaptation) and Mr.Hisum (64.19 mAP@50), utilizing a novel Dynamic SinkCache mechanism for constant memory usage and an uncertainty-aware scoring function.

title: AHA -- Predicting What Matters Next: Online Highlight Detection Without Looking Ahead

abstract: Real-time streaming video understanding in domains such as autonomous driving and intelligent surveillance poses challenges beyond conventional offline video processing, requiring continuous perception, proactive decision making, and responsive interaction based on dynamically evolving visual content. However, existing methods rely on alternating perception-reaction or asynchronous triggers, lacking task-driven planning and future anticipation, which limits their real-time responsiveness and proactive decision making in evolving video streams. To this end, we propose a StreamAgent that anticipates the temporal intervals and spatial regions expected to contain future task-relevant information to enable proactive and goal-driven responses. Specifically, we integrate question semantics and historical observations through prompting the anticipatory agent to anticipate the temporal progression of key events, align current observations with the expected future evidence, and subsequently adjust the perception action (e.g., attending to task-relevant regions or continuously tracking in subsequent frames). To enable efficient inference, we design a streaming KV-cache memory mechanism that constructs a hierarchical memory structure for selective recall of relevant tokens, enabling efficient semantic retrieval while reducing the overhead of storing all tokens in the traditional KV-cache. Extensive experiments on streaming and long video understanding tasks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing methods in response accuracy and real-time efficiency, highlighting its practical value for real-world streaming scenarios.
title: StreamAgent: Towards Anticipatory Agents for Streaming Video Understanding

abstract: Recent researches on video large language models (VideoLLM) predominantly focus on model architectures and training datasets, leaving the interaction format between the user and the model under-explored. In existing works, users often interact with VideoLLMs by using the entire video and a query as input, after which the model generates a response. This interaction format constrains the application of VideoLLMs in scenarios such as live-streaming comprehension where videos do not end and responses are required in a real-time manner, and also results in unsatisfactory performance on time-sensitive tasks that requires localizing video segments. In this paper, we focus on a video-text duet interaction format. This interaction format is characterized by the continuous playback of the video, and both the user and the model can insert their text messages at any position during the video playback. When a text message ends, the video continues to play, akin to the alternative of two performers in a duet. We construct MMDuetIT, a video-text training dataset designed to adapt VideoLLMs to video-text duet interaction format. We also introduce the Multi-Answer Grounded Video Question Answering (MAGQA) task to benchmark the real-time response ability of VideoLLMs. Trained on MMDuetIT, MMDuet demonstrates that adopting the video-text duet interaction format enables the model to achieve significant improvements in various time-sensitive tasks (76% CIDEr on YouCook2 dense video captioning, 90\% mAP on QVHighlights highlight detection and 25% R@0.5 on Charades-STA temporal video grounding) with minimal training efforts, and also enable VideoLLMs to reply in a real-time manner as the video plays.

organization_info.name: Beijing Institute for General Artificial Intelligence
State Key Laboratory of General Artificial Intelligence

title: VideoLLM Knows When to Speak: Enhancing Time-Sensitive Video Comprehension with Video-Text Duet Interaction Format

abstract: Recent researches on video large language models (VideoLLM) predominantly focus on model architectures and training datasets, leaving the interaction format between the user and the model under-explored. In existing works, users often interact with VideoLLMs by using the entire video and a query as input, after which the model generates a response. This interaction format constrains the application of VideoLLMs in scenarios such as live-streaming comprehension where videos do not end and responses are required in a real-time manner, and also results in unsatisfactory performance on time-sensitive tasks that requires localizing video segments. In this paper, we focus on a video-text duet interaction format. This interaction format is characterized by the continuous playback of the video, and both the user and the model can insert their text messages at any position during the video playback. When a text message ends, the video continues to play, akin to the alternative of two performers in a duet. We construct MMDuetIT, a video-text training dataset designed to adapt VideoLLMs to video-text duet interaction format. We also introduce the Multi-Answer Grounded Video Question Answering (MAGQA) task to benchmark the real-time response ability of VideoLLMs. Trained on MMDuetIT, MMDuet demonstrates that adopting the video-text duet interaction format enables the model to achieve significant improvements in various time-sensitive tasks (76% CIDEr on YouCook2 dense video captioning, 90\% mAP on QVHighlights highlight detection and 25% R@0.5 on Charades-STA temporal video grounding) with minimal training efforts, and also enable VideoLLMs to reply in a real-time manner as the video plays.

keyInsights: Empowering a VideoLLM to interject responses during continuous video playback enhances its capacity for fine-grained, time-sensitive video comprehension and precise temporal grounding.
Integrating specialized "Informative" and "Relevance" decision-making heads allows the model to proactively determine the appropriate moments to generate descriptive or query-relevant text responses.
Dynamically managing the KV cache by removing past model outputs during real-time generation improves response quality by reducing repetition and allowing the LLM to focus on new video content.
originalProblem: Existing Video Large Language Models (VideoLLMs) primarily rely on a "whole video interaction format," which processes entire video content before generating a response.
This approach restricts VideoLLMs from real-time applications like live-streaming comprehension, which require dynamic and timely interactions.
Traditional methods perform unsatisfactorily on time-sensitive tasks that demand precise temporal localization, such as highlight detection and dense video captioning.
results: MMDuet showed improved performance on highlight detection and temporal video grounding, achieving 31.3 mAP on QVHighlights and 42.4 R@0.5 on Charades-STA, outperforming baselines.
For dense video captioning, MMDuet recorded higher CIDEr (8.8) and SODAc (2.9) scores on YouCook2, benefiting from its real-time response capability.

The model exhibited superior real-time, multi-turn answering on the MAGQA task and achieved 29.44% accuracy in proactive output on StreamingBench, confirming its ability to provide timely, scene-aware responses.
solution: A "video-text duet interaction format" is introduced, allowing dynamic, real-time turn-taking where the user, model, and continuous video stream can interject text messages.
The MMDuet model architecture integrates two decision-making heads (Informative and Relevance) into a VideoLLM to predict the optimal moment for generating responses during video playback.

A dedicated training dataset, MMDuetIT, and a new task, Multi-Answer Grounded Video Question Answering (MAGQA), were created to facilitate the model's adaptation to this real-time interaction paradigm.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Peking University, Huawei Noah's Ark Lab, and other institutions developed MMDuet, a Video Large Language Model that employs a video-text duet interaction format to enhance real-time, time-sensitive video comprehension.
